input.case_summary: This case originated from letters to the Chief Justice of India highlighting deaths in police custody. The court treated these letters as writ petitions under public interest litigation. The petitioners sought accountability for custodial deaths and compensation for victims. The respondents, various state governments, initially denied any wrongdoing. The Supreme Court, concerned about increasing custodial violence, formulated guidelines to minimize such incidents and ensure compensation for victims' families.
input.legal_issue: Whether custodial violence, including torture and death in police lock-ups, violates the fundamental rights guaranteed under Articles 21 and 22 of the Constitution of India, and if so, what measures are necessary to protect these rights and provide redressal to victims.
input.relevant_laws: Article 21 Constitution of India, Article 22 Constitution of India, Article 20(3) Constitution of India, Article 5 Universal Declaration of Human Rights, Section 41 Cr.P.C., Section 46 Cr.P.C., Section 49 Cr.P.C., Section 50 Cr.P.C., Section 56 Cr.P.C., Section 57 Cr.P.C., Section 176 Cr.P.C.
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the present petitions suffer from a fundamental procedural infirmity, as the letters addressed to the Chief Justice of India were not accompanied by a verified affidavit as required under the Supreme Court Rules, 1966. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to draw attention to the well-settled principle that public interest litigation must be initiated through a properly sworn affidavit to prevent misuse of the process. My Lords, the counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to Rule 1 of Order XXXVIII of the Supreme Court Rules, which mandates that every petition shall be supported by an affidavit. This requirement is essential to establish the bonafides of the petitioner and cannot be waived merely because the subject matter involves allegations of custodial violence. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you point out a procedural infirmity in your own petition — are you conceding maintainability?
output.judge_notes: Petitioner voluntarily raises procedural flaw — curious tactic, likely wants Court to waive it

input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you point out a procedural infirmity in your own petition — are you conceding maintainability?
input.current_argument: My Lords, in response to the question posed by the Hon'ble bench, the counsel humbly submits that pointing to a procedural infirmity is not a concession of maintainability but rather an acknowledgment that the court may—in its extraordinary jurisdiction—overlook procedural rigours when fundamental rights of such magnitude are at stake. My Lords, the counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the settled distinction between a procedural defect, which can be cured or waived in the interest of justice, and a lack of subject-matter jurisdiction, which this court clearly possesses under Article 32 read with Articles 21 and 22. The very fact that this court treated the petitioners' letters as writ petitions under public interest litigation, and has proceeded to hear the merits, demonstrates that maintainability is founded on the substantive violation of constitutional rights, not on technical adherence to pleading formalities.

output.judge_response: Counsel, what precisely is the procedural infirmity you refer to, and under which authority can this court waive it?
output.judge_notes: Handled the maintainability question well but avoided citing specific precedent—watch for follow-up on which case permits waiver of procedural rules for fundamental rights.

input.case_summary: This case originated from letters to the Chief Justice of India highlighting deaths in police custody. input.judge_last_response: Counsel, what precisely is the procedural infirmity you refer to, and under which authority can this court waive it?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the procedural infirmity lies in the utter failure to comply with the mandatory requirements of Sections 57 and 160 of the Criminal Procedure Code, which mandate production before a magistrate within 24 hours and prohibit detention in uncomfortable surroundings. In Nilabati Behera v. State of Orissa, this Hon’ble Court established that custodial violence directly violates Article 21 and that the State bears strict liability for compensation. Applying that ratio, the present cases of death in custody, absent any magisterial oversight, demonstrate a clear constitutional violation. The State may argue that internal departmental action suffices, but such measures are patently inadequate to address the systemic breach of fundamental rights. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you have not addressed the second limb of the question — on what basis can this court waive these procedural requirements?
output.judge_notes: Counsel correctly identified procedural infirmity but failed to address the authority for waiver — needs to ground it in constitutional mandate.

input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you have not addressed the second limb of the question — on what basis can this court waive these procedural requirements?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that this Hon’ble Court has repeatedly and unequivocally asserted its inherent power to waive procedural requirements when fundamental rights are at stake—Maneka Gandhi, Olga Tellis, and Vishaka are but a few luminous authorities that stand for that very proposition. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel draws the attention of the Hon’ble bench to the fact that in the present case, the spectre of custodial violence under Articles 21 and 22 demands no less than the same robust exercise of jurisdiction. The counsel further invites Your Lordships to recall the very name of this case—D.K. Basu itself is a monument to the principle that procedure cannot be permitted to become a dead letter when life and liberty hang in the balance. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, if the State had provided some procedural safeguards short of full compliance, would your inherent waiver argument still hold?
output.judge_notes: Cited cases but didn't explain ratio or apply to waiver specifics — needs to tie Maneka Gandhi's procedural reasonableness to custodial context.

input.relevant_laws: Article 21 Constitution of India, Article 22 Constitution of India, Article 20(3) Constitution of India, Article 5 Universal Declaration of Human Rights, Section 41 Cr.P.C., Section 46 Cr.P.C., Section 49 Cr.P.C., Section 50 Cr.P.C., Section 56 Cr.P.C., Section 57 Cr.P.C., Section 176 Cr.P.C.
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, if the State had provided some procedural safeguards short of full compliance, would your inherent waiver argument still hold?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that if the State had indeed provided some procedural safeguards, even short of full compliance, the inherent waiver argument might lose its force. For where the State takes any steps toward accountability, it effectively acknowledges the existence of the rights at stake, and thus the doctrine of waiver could be said to apply only to a complete failure of process. This suggests that a partial safeguard, however inadequate, may preclude the automatic assumption of a constitutional violation that the petitioners seek to establish. The court may therefore find that the threshold for a per se violation of Articles 21 and 22 is not met in every case where only minor procedural lapses occur. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the respondent on this point.
output.judge_notes: Counsel conceded that partial safeguards might negate per se violation — dangerous for petitioner's case.

input.relevant_laws: Article 21 Constitution of India, Article 22 Constitution of India, Article 20(3) Constitution of India, Article 5 Universal Declaration of Human Rights, Section 41 Cr.P.C., Section 46 Cr.P.C., Section 49 Cr.P.C., Section 50 Cr.P.C., Section 56 Cr.P.C., Section 57 Cr.P.C., Section 176 Cr.P.C.
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you list the statutory provisions but after Maneka Gandhi, procedure under Article 21 must be just, fair, and reasonable — how do these sections meet that standard in the context of custodial violence?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the statutory framework under the Code of Criminal Procedure, including Sections 41, 46, 49, 50, 56, 57, and 176, was meticulously crafted to regulate police conduct during arrest and custody. These provisions, when duly observed, establish a procedure that by its very design aims to safeguard individual liberty and dignity. In the present matters, the respondent States have repeatedly affirmed their adherence to this framework, ensuring that every arrest is documented, every detainee is informed of grounds, and every custodial event is subject to magisterial inquiry under Section 176. The counsel draws the attention of the Hon’ble bench to the fact that the alleged incidents, if any, are isolated deviations from this robust statutory scheme, not reflections of its inadequacy. Thus, the procedure prescribed, when faithfully implemented, is just, fair, and reasonable within the meaning of Article 21 as interpreted in Maneka Gandhi. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel submits that the existing provisions, coupled with administrative oversight, meet the constitutional standard. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, if all statutory formalities are scrupulously observed yet a detainee is tortured, does the procedure remain just, fair, and reasonable under Article 21?
output.judge_notes: Counsel gave a textbook answer but didn't actually answer the judge's question — just reiterated that procedures exist and are followed

input.relevant_laws: Article 21 Constitution of India, Article 22 Constitution of India, Article 20(3) Constitution of India, Article 5 Universal Declaration of Human Rights, Section 41 Cr.P.C., Section 46 Cr.P.C., Section 49 Cr.P.C., Section 50 Cr.P.C., Section 56 Cr.P.C., Section 57 Cr.P.C., Section 176 Cr.P.C.
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, if all statutory formalities are scrupulously observed yet a detainee is tortured, does the procedure remain just, fair, and reasonable under Article 21?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that if all statutory formalities under Sections 41, 46, 49, 50, 56, and 57 of the Code of Criminal Procedure are scrupulously observed, the procedure must be presumed just, fair, and reasonable under Article 21. The counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the settled principle in *A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras* that "procedure established by law" means any procedure duly enacted by the State, and compliance with that procedure satisfies the constitutional mandate. In the present case, the respondent States have followed every statutory safeguard, and any subsequent act of torture is an individual aberration, not a failure of the procedure itself. Thus, the procedure remains constitutionally valid even if a detainee suffers harm, for Article 21 does not guarantee perfect outcomes, only lawful process. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to proceed.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the petitioner's response on this point.
output.judge_notes: Counsel relied on overruled A.K. Gopalan to justify torture — legally indefensible.

input.case_summary: This case involves a batch of petitions challenging the constitutional validity of Article 323A and 323B of the Constitution, which established administrative tribunals. The central issue is whether these tribunals, designed to handle specific disputes, unconstitutionally exclude the High Courts' power of judicial review under Articles 226/227 and the Supreme Court's power under Article 32. The petitioners argued that the tribunals, as structured, undermine the constitutional scheme of judicial review. The Supreme Court was asked to determine the extent of the tribunals' jurisdiction and their impact on the High Courts' powers.
input.legal_issue: Does the power conferred upon Parliament or State Legislatures by Article 323A(2)(d) or Article 323B(3)(d) of the Constitution to exclude the jurisdiction of all courts, except the Supreme Court under Article 136, in respect of disputes and complaints referred to in Article 323A(1) or Article 323B(2), run counter to the power of judicial review conferred on the High Courts under Articles 226/227 and on the Supreme Court under Article 32 of the Constitution?
input.relevant_laws: Article 32 Constitution of India, Article 136 Constitution of India, Article 226 Constitution of India, Article 227 Constitution of India, Article 323A Constitution of India, Article 323B Constitution of India, Article 31A Constitution of India, Article 309 Constitution of India, Article 329 Constitution of India, Article 329A Constitution of India, Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 16(1) Constitution of India, Section 5(6) Administrative Tribunals Act, 1985, Section 6 Administrative Tribunals Act, 1985, Section 28 Administrative Tribunals Act, 1985, Industrial Disputes Act, 1947
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the power to exclude the jurisdiction of High Courts under Articles 323A and 323B may be constitutionally permissible if the alternative tribunal mechanism provides an equally efficacious remedy and retains the ultimate supervisory jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court under Article 136. The counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to Articles 31A and 329 of the Constitution, which themselves carve out limited exceptions to the ordinary scope of judicial review. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel concedes that the basic structure of judicial review does not require the High Courts to be the sole or primary forum in every class of disputes, provided the substitute is adequate and the Supreme Court’s overarching power remains intact. Thus, the mere creation of a specialised tribunal need not automatically violate the constitutional scheme.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you appear to concede what the petitioner's case seeks to deny — that ouster of High Court jurisdiction is permissible under certain conditions. Is this not a departure from the settled position that judicial review under Articles 226 and 32 is part of the basic structure?
output.judge_notes: Counsel conceded core point in first submission — weak position for petitioner

input.case_summary: This case involves a batch of petitions challenging the constitutional validity of Article 323A and 323B of the Constitution, which established administrative tribunals. The central issue is whether these tribunals, designed to handle specific disputes, unconstitutionally exclude the High Courts' power of judicial review under Articles 226/227 and the Supreme Court's power under Article 32. The petitioners argued that the tribunals, as structured, undermine the constitutional scheme of judicial review. The Supreme Court was asked to determine the extent of the tribunals' jurisdiction and their impact on the High Courts' powers.
input.legal_issue: Does the power conferred upon Parliament or State Legislatures by Article 323A(2)(d) or Article 323B(3)(d) of the Constitution to exclude the jurisdiction of all courts, except the Supreme Court under Article 136, in respect of disputes and complaints referred to in Article 323A(1) or Article 323B(2), run counter to the power of judicial review conferred on the High Courts under Articles 226/227 and on the Supreme Court under Article 32 of the Constitution?
input.relevant_laws: Article 32 Constitution of India, Article 136 Constitution of India, Article 226 Constitution of India, Article 227 Constitution of India, Article 323A Constitution of India, Article 323B Constitution of India, Article 31A Constitution of India, Article 309 Constitution of India, Article 329 Constitution of India, Article 329A Constitution of India, Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 16(1) Constitution of India, Section 5(6) Administrative Tribunals Act, 1985, Section 6 Administrative Tribunals Act, 1985, Section 28 Administrative Tribunals Act, 1985, Industrial Disputes Act, 1947
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you appear to concede what the petitioner's case seeks to deny — that ouster of High Court jurisdiction is permissible under certain conditions. Is this not a departure from the settled position that judicial review under Articles 226 and 32 is part of the basic structure?
input.current_argument: My Lords, in response to the question posed by the Hon'ble bench, the counsel humbly submits that the petitioner does not concede any general permissibility to oust High Court jurisdiction. The distinction, Your Lordships, lies in the degree of ouster: while limited ousters for specialized tribunals may be sustained if they retain a mechanism for judicial review by the High Court, the impugned provisions under Articles 323A and 323B completely exclude the High Court's power under Articles 226 and 227, leaving only a discretionary appeal under Article 136. This total exclusion, the counsel submits, strikes at the very core of the basic structure, as judicial review by constitutional courts cannot be supplanted by tribunals that lack the same comprehensive powers. My Lords, the petitioner's position remains that any ouster which abrogates the High Court's constitutional role as the sentinel of justice is impermissible, and the present case falls squarely within that prohibition.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the respondent.
output.judge_notes: Sharp response — neatly distinguished limited ouster from total exclusion, directly answered the judge's concern.

output.judge_notes: Cites multiple cases but none applied specifically — relies heavily on Article 136 but ignores distinction from Articles 226/227 as remedies of right.

input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you rely on Article 136 as preserving judicial review, but is review under Article 136 discretionary while Articles 226 and 227 provide remedies as of right? How does your argument reconcile with that distinction?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the discretionary nature of Article 136 does not, in the submission of the respondent, detract from the constitutional sufficiency of the remedy it provides. The Hon'ble bench is respectfully invited to consider that the very existence of this appellate jurisdiction, as preserved under the impugned provisions, ensures that the essence of judicial review remains intact—even if the mode of its exercise differs from the plenary writ jurisdiction of the High Courts. The counsel draws the attention of the court to the fact that the tribunals are designed to substitute, not eliminate, the High Courts' role in the specified categories of disputes, and the safety valve of Article 136 stands as a bulwark. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to proceed on the basis that the distinction between a remedy as of right and a discretionary one does not, in the face of the clear text of Articles 323A and 323B, render the scheme unconstitutional. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, if Article 136 is discretionary, how does a litigant who cannot obtain special leave secure judicial review of a tribunal decision that may be patently illegal?
output.judge_notes: Counsel gave a polite but superficial answer; did not engage with the discretionary/remedy distinction substantively.

Sampath Kumar v. Union of India, where this Court upheld tribunals as valid substitutes for High Courts provided they adhere to the principles of judicial review. The availability of Article 136, coupled with this Court’s inherent power to grant leave in exceptional cases, ensures that no litigant is left without a remedy for a patently illegal tribunal decision. If Your Lordships are concerned that discretionary leave may be denied, the counsel respectfully submits that the tribunal itself is empowered to correct its own errors through review and that this Court’s supervisory role under Article 136 is constitutionally adequate to uphold the rule of law. One might argue that discretionary relief is uncertain, but this Court’s well-settled test for granting special leave—focused on substantial questions of law and justice—provides a sufficient safeguard against illegality.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you say the Court's test for special leave is a sufficient safeguard, but if a litigant's case does not meet that test even though the tribunal decision is patently illegal, what then?
output.judge_notes: Counsel relied on S.P. Sampath Kumar but didn't address the gap: discretionary leave may be denied even for patent illegalities. The answer was confident but not airtight.

The Supreme Court was asked to determine the extent of the tribunals' jurisdiction and their impact on the High Courts' powers.

Raghuraj Singh held that Article 136 is a residuary power intended to correct grave miscarriages of justice. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the fact that a patently illegal tribunal decision necessarily constitutes such a grave miscarriage, thereby squarely falling within this Court's purview. Much obliged, My Lords. The respondent submits that the availability of special leave under Article 136 is therefore a complete safeguard, and no additional layer of High Court review under Article 226 is constitutionally required.

output.judge_notes: Counsel cites Durga Shankar Mehta correctly but glosses over the discretionary nature of Article 136 — a patently illegal decision may still be denied leave.

input.case_summary: Maneka Gandhi's passport was impounded by the government under Section 10(3)(c) of the Passports Act, 1967, citing 'public interest.' She was denied the reasons for the impoundment. Gandhi challenged this action, arguing it violated her fundamental rights. The Supreme Court held that the right to travel abroad is part of personal liberty under Article 21, and any procedure restricting it must be fair, just, and reasonable. The court also ruled that Articles 19 and 21 are not mutually exclusive and that laws impacting personal liberty must also meet the requirements of Article 19.
input.legal_issue: Whether Section 10(3)(c) of the Passports Act, 1967, and the action of impounding a passport without providing reasons, violates Articles 14, 19(1)(a), and 21 of the Constitution of India.
input.relevant_laws: Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 19(1)(a) Constitution of India, Article 21 Constitution of India, Passports Act, 1967, Section 3 Passports Act, 1967, Section 5 Passports Act, 1967, Section 6 Passports Act, 1967, Section 10 Passports Act, 1967
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the respondent's reliance on 'public interest' as a blanket justification for impounding the passport without reasons is entirely misplaced. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the fact that the mere invocation of a vague term cannot satisfy the rigorous test of fairness under Article 21. The counsel submits that the respondent has not demonstrated how providing reasons would actually harm any specific public interest, leaving the petitioner with no means to defend her liberty. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, if the statute explicitly empowers impoundment in the interest of the general public, on what basis do you argue that reasons must be disclosed?
output.judge_notes: Petitioner argues against blanket 'public interest' but hasn't addressed Section 10(3)(c) text — can she read down the provision?

input.case_summary: Maneka Gandhi's passport was impounded by the government under Section 10(3)(c) of the Passports Act, 1967, citing 'public interest.' She was denied the reasons for the impoundment. Gandhi challenged this action, arguing it violated her fundamental rights. The Supreme Court held that the right to travel abroad is part of personal liberty under Article 21, and any procedure restricting it must be fair, just, and reasonable. The court also ruled that Articles 19 and 21 are not mutually exclusive and that laws impacting personal liberty must also meet the requirements of Article 19.
input.legal_issue: Whether Section 10(3)(c) of the Passports Act, 1967, and the action of impounding a passport without providing reasons, violates Articles 14, 19(1)(a), and 21 of the Constitution of India.
input.relevant_laws: Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 19(1)(a) Constitution of India, Article 21 Constitution of India, Passports Act, 1967, Section 3 Passports Act, 1967, Section 5 Passports Act, 1967, Section 6 Passports Act, 1967, Section 10 Passports Act, 1967
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, if the statute explicitly empowers impoundment in the interest of the general public, on what basis do you argue that reasons must be disclosed?
input.current_argument: My Lords, in response to the question posed by the Hon'ble bench, the counsel humbly submits that while the statute does empower impoundment in the interest of the general public, such empowerment does not absolve the State from complying with the fundamental requirement of procedural fairness under Article 21. My Lords, the counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the settled principle that the procedure established by law must be fair, just, and reasonable, and a power exercised without disclosure of reasons becomes inherently arbitrary and renders the restriction upon personal liberty insupportable. The distinction lies between the existence of a power and the manner of its exercise: even a validly conferred power must be exercised with transparency to allow for judicial review and to prevent the very mischief of unreasonableness that the Constitution prohibits. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to submit that the impoundment without reasons violates the golden triangle of Articles 14, 19, and 21, and thus the statute cannot protect such action. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the respondent on the public interest justification for withholding reasons.
output.judge_notes: Excellent response - directly answered the bench question, correctly applied Maneka Gandhi's fair procedure requirement, and distinguished between existence and exercise of power. Ready to hear respondent's defense.

input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the core requirement under Article 21 is that any procedure restricting personal liberty must be fair, just, and reasonable. In Maneka Gandhi itself, this Hon'ble Court held that even if a statute prescribes a procedure, it must not be arbitrary or oppressive, and that the right to travel abroad is part of personal liberty. Merely providing a "brief reason" without any opportunity for the petitioner to respond or seek judicial review would still fall short of the standard of reasonableness, as the procedure would lack the essential element of natural justice. Even if national security concerns are invoked, the authority must at least disclose sufficient reasons to enable a court to assess the proportionality of the restriction, otherwise the order remains a bald assertion immune from scrutiny. The counsel contends that the true test is not the brevity of the reason but whether the procedure as a whole is arbitrary; a cryptic reason that cannot be effectively challenged still violates Articles 14 and 21. Much obliged, My Lords.

input.case_summary: A.K. Gopalan, detained under the Preventive Detention Act, 1950, petitioned the Supreme Court for a writ of habeas corpus, arguing the Act violated his fundamental rights under Articles 13, 19, 21, and 22 of the Constitution. He claimed the Act was unconstitutional and his detention illegal. The Court upheld the validity of the Preventive Detention Act, except for Section 14, which restricted disclosure of detention grounds to the court. The majority held that Article 19 doesn't apply to laws of preventive detention, and the Act didn't violate Articles 21 and 22.
input.legal_issue: Does the Preventive Detention Act, 1950, violate fundamental rights guaranteed under Articles 13, 19, 21, and 22 of the Constitution of India, specifically concerning personal liberty and freedom of movement?
input.relevant_laws: Article 13 Constitution of India, Article 19 Constitution of India, Article 21 Constitution of India, Article 22 Constitution of India, Article 32 Constitution of India, Preventive Detention Act, 1950, Section 3 Preventive Detention Act, 1950, Section 7 Preventive Detention Act, 1950, Section 11 Preventive Detention Act, 1950, Section 12 Preventive Detention Act, 1950, Section 14 Preventive Detention Act, 1950
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the Preventive Detention Act, 1950, violates Article 20(1) of the Constitution, as it imposes a custodial penalty on the petitioner for conduct that was not an offence under any law in force at the time of his alleged acts. The Act’s preventive mechanism, by its very nature, penalizes the detainee without a prior criminal law defining the prohibited behaviour, thereby offending the guarantee against ex post facto legislation. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel draws the attention of the Hon’ble bench to the settled principle that any law which subjects a person to deprivation of liberty as a punitive measure must rest on a pre-existing statutory prohibition—a requirement wholly absent here. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you invoke Article 20(1) against a preventive detention law — can you explain how preventive detention, which is not punitive, attracts the prohibition against ex post facto legislation?
output.judge_notes: Petitioner misapplied Article 20(1) — preventive detention is not punitive; argument legally unsound.

input.current_argument: My Lords, in response to the question posed by the Hon'ble bench, the counsel humbly submits that while preventive detention is indeed non-punitive in form, the application of Article 20(1) is not limited by the label of "punitive" but by the substance of the law's operation. The Preventive Detention Act permits detention based on past conduct, as the grounds for detention necessarily derive from prior acts of the detainee, thereby imposing a penalty for conduct that was lawful when committed. The counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the fact that Section 3 of the Act empowers the detaining authority to act on "any act" done previously, rendering it a retrospective penal measure in substance.

input.relevant_laws: Article 13 Constitution of India, Article 19 Constitution of India, Article 21 Constitution of India, Article 22 Constitution of India, Article 32 Constitution of India, Preventive Detention Act, 1950, Section 3 Preventive Detention Act, 1950, Section 7 Preventive Detention Act, 1950, Section 11 Preventive Detention Act, 1950, Section 12 Preventive Detention Act, 1950, Section 14 Preventive Detention Act, 1950
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, if the detention order is based on a reasonable apprehension of future harmful conduct rather than solely past acts, does your Article 20(1) argument still hold?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that even where a detention order is based on a reasonable apprehension of future harmful conduct, the argument under Article 20(1) remains tenable. It is well settled that Article 20(1) protects against the imposition of any penal consequence for conduct that was not an offence when committed, and the law recognizes that the substance of the restraint, not its label, determines whether it is punitive in effect. The Preventive Detention Act, by treating mere suspicion of future acts as a ground for incarceration, effectively imposes a penalty without a defined past offence, thereby attracting the protective principle of Article 20(1). My Lords, the counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the cardinal rule that fundamental rights are not to be frustrated by semantic distinctions, and thus the absence of a completed criminal act does not insulate the law from constitutional challenge under Article 20(1).

output.judge_notes: Counsel attempted to stretch Article 20(1) to preventive detention, but the ratio of Gopalan clearly held it inapplicable. Weak on substance but responsive.

The Court upheld the validity of the Preventive Detention Act, except for Section 14, which restricted disclosure of detention grounds to the court. The majority held that Article 19 doesn't apply to laws of preventive detention, and the Act didn't violate Articles 21 and 22.
input.legal_issue: Does the Preventive Detention Act, 1950, violate fundamental rights guaranteed under Articles 13, 19, 21, and 22 of the Constitution of India, specifically concerning personal liberty and freedom of movement?

input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you rely on Article 21 but have not addressed the specific safeguards under Article 22 — what is your submission on their interplay?

Article 22 is a self-contained code for preventive detention, providing its own safeguards, while Article 21 deals with procedure established by law for deprivation of liberty. The opposing counsel mistakenly conflates the two, but there is no requirement that procedural fairness under Article 21 be read into Article 22. input.case_summary: This case involves allegations of bribery and corruption in the purchase of guns from the Swedish company A.B. Before by the Indian government. A First Information Report (FIR) was registered by the CBI, and a letter rogatory was issued to Switzerland for investigation. H.S. Chowdhary, claiming to be a public interest litigant, challenged the FIR and the letter rogatory. The High Court dismissed his petition for lack of locus standi, and the Supreme Court upheld that decision, finding no grounds to interfere with the investigation.
input.legal_issue: Does a person claiming to be a public interest litigant have locus standi to challenge the registration of a First Information Report (FIR) and the issuance of a letter rogatory in a criminal investigation, particularly when the investigation is at a preliminary stage?
input.relevant_laws: Article 51(A) Constitution of India, Section 120-B IPC, Sections 161, 162, 163, 164, 165-A IPC, Sections 5(2), 5(1)(d), 5(2)/5(1)(c) Prevention of Corruption Act, 1947, Sections 409, 420, 468, 471 IPC, Sections 397, 482 CrPC, Sections 119, 401 CrPC, Section 340 Cr. P.C.
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the issuance of a letter rogatory without first establishing dual criminality violates the fundamental principles of international comity and mutual legal assistance. The counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the settled principle that no state should execute a request for evidence unless the conduct in question constitutes an offence under the laws of both the requesting and the requested jurisdictions. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to proceed by arguing that this preliminary jurisdictional hurdle is a sine qua non for any such rogatory commission, and its omission renders the entire investigation tainted from inception. Therefore, the letter rogatory in the present matter must be treated as void ab initio, and the FIR cannot stand upon such defective foundation. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you argue about dual criminality, but the preliminary issue is whether you have locus standi at all. How does your submission address that?
output.judge_notes: Counsel jumped straight to merits of letter rogatory without establishing standing — appears off-point.

input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you argue about dual criminality, but the preliminary issue is whether you have locus standi at all. How does your submission address that?
input.current_argument: My Lords, in response to the question posed by the Hon'ble bench, the counsel humbly submits that the issue of dual criminality directly informs the petitioner's locus standi, because a public interest litigant has standing to challenge the legality of a letter rogatory that would bypass fundamental jurisdictional safeguards. The petitioner does not seek to halt the investigation on its merits, but to prevent an unlawful procedural overreach that could vitiate the entire inquiry—a harm that affects the public at large, not merely any individual. The distinction lies between interfering with an investigation and ensuring its compliance with legal prerequisites, and the courts have recognized PIL standing where such jurisdictional errors threaten the rule of law. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel draws attention to the principle that locus standi is broader when the challenge is to the very authority of the investigating agency to act, rather than to the evidence gathered. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you speak of jurisdictional errors but have not cited a single authority where this court recognized PIL standing to challenge a letter rogatory at the preliminary stage. What is your submission on that?
output.judge_notes: Counsel attempted to link dual criminality to standing but failed to cite any authority; argument is plausible but lacks legal grounding.

input.case_summary: This case involves allegations of bribery and corruption in the purchase of guns from the Swedish company A.B. Before by the Indian government. input.relevant_laws: Article 51(A) Constitution of India, Section 120-B IPC, Sections 161, 162, 163, 164, 165-A IPC, Sections 5(2), 5(1)(d), 5(2)/5(1)(c) Prevention of Corruption Act, 1947, Sections 409, 420, 468, 471 IPC, Sections 397, 482 CrPC, Sections 119, 401 CrPC, Section 340 Cr. P.C.
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you cite broad PIL jurisprudence, but can you take me to a decision where the standing of a third party to challenge the registration of an FIR was upheld?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the decision in Vineet Narain v. Union of India, (1998) 1 SCC 226. In that case, this Court entertained a public interest petition from a third party who had no personal interest, challenging the manner of investigation by the CBI in a corruption case, which necessarily involved scrutiny of the FIR. The ratio of that case establishes that a public-spirited citizen has standing to question the registration of an FIR if it is shown to be arbitrary or mala fide. Applying that principle here, the petitioner, acting as a public interest litigant, is entitled to challenge the registration of the FIR and the letter rogatory, especially since the investigation suffers from procedural irregularities. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to proceed.

output.judge_response: Counsel, in Vineet Narain the court was monitoring an ongoing investigation, not adjudicating on the validity of an FIR registration. Does that distinction affect your submission?
output.judge_notes: Case cited is about supervisory jurisdiction, not standing to challenge FIR registration; not precisely on point to the judge's question.

input.case_summary: This case involves allegations of bribery and corruption in the purchase of guns from the Swedish company A.B. Before by the Indian government. input.relevant_laws: Article 51(A) Constitution of India, Section 120-B IPC, Sections 161, 162, 163, 164, 165-A IPC, Sections 5(2), 5(1)(d), 5(2)/5(1)(c) Prevention of Corruption Act, 1947, Sections 409, 420, 468, 471 IPC, Sections 397, 482 CrPC, Sections 119, 401 CrPC, Section 340 Cr. P.C.
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, in Vineet Narain the court was monitoring an ongoing investigation, not adjudicating on the validity of an FIR registration. Does that distinction affect your submission?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the distinction drawn by Your Lordships is instructive but ultimately reinforces the petitioner's standing. In Vineet Narain, this Hon'ble Court exercised its supervisory jurisdiction precisely because the investigation was ongoing, recognizing that judicial oversight is most critical when proceedings are nascent and susceptible to abuse. The counsel concedes that the petitioner here challenges the FIR and letter rogatory at the same preliminary stage, and thus the principle of non-interference with investigations in progress must apply with equal force. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel acknowledges that this timing may actually militate against granting the relief sought, as the Court's role is to monitor, not to halt, an inquiry at its threshold.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the respondent.
output.judge_notes: Counsel conceded that the distinction works against his own argument, undermining his standing claim.

Union of India does not weaken the requisite of bona fides in public interest litigation; rather, that case permitted intervention only because of demonstrable investigative failure, whereas here the CBI is actively and competently pursuing the probe. The present matter, governed by the locus standi principle under Article 51-A read with the scheme of the CrPC, requires the petitioner to show a direct interest or special injury, which he manifestly lacks. The counterargument that Vineet Narain grants any citizen a right to challenge any FIR or letter rogatory is misplaced—that judgment carefully restricted its application to extraordinary failures, not to routine investigative steps. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel submits that the bona fides of the petitioner are suspect precisely because he seeks to obstruct, not advance, the investigation, a distinction that does not affect but rather reinforces the established principle.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you argue the petitioner's bona fides are suspect because he seeks to obstruct the investigation, but does the mere act of challenging an FIR automatically indicate obstruction, or must the court examine the specific allegations in the petition to assess mala fides?
output.judge_notes: Counsel effectively distinguished Vineet Narain on facts, but the claim that challenging FIR equals obstruction may need more support.

Thus, Your Lordships, the Article 21 argument remains fully intact even if the transfer was purportedly issued under plenary powers.

input.case_summary: Several petitioners challenged the validity of admissions to the Regional Engineering College, Srinagar, arguing that the college, registered as a society, was acting arbitrarily in its admission process. The petitioners contended that the college should be considered a 'State' under Article 12 of the Constitution and thus subject to fundamental rights. The Supreme Court held that the college was indeed a 'State' due to the deep and pervasive control of the Central and State governments, but ultimately dismissed the writ petitions, finding some aspects of the admission process arbitrary but declining to overturn the admissions due to the advanced stage of the academic year.
input.legal_issue: Whether the Regional Engineering College, Srinagar, registered as a society under the Jammu & Kashmir Registration of Societies Act, 1898, is a 'State' within the meaning of Article 12 of the Constitution of India and therefore subject to the fundamental rights guaranteed under Part III of the Constitution.
input.relevant_laws: Article 12 Constitution of India, Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 32 Constitution of India, Article 36 Constitution of India, Articles 309 Constitution of India, Articles 310 Constitution of India, Articles 311 Constitution of India, Jammu & Kashmir Registration of Societies Act, 1898, Societies Registration Act, 1860, Companies Act, 1956
input.current_argument: My Lords, the opposing counsel argues that the mere registration of the college as a society under the Societies Registration Act removes it from the ambit of Article 12. However, the counsel humbly submits that this is a deeply flawed proposition, as the test is not the form of registration but the reality of governmental control. My Lords, the fact that the entire share capital is held by the Central and State governments is alone sufficient to bring the college within the definition of 'State'. The counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the name 'Regional Engineering College' itself—this nomenclature inherently signals a public, State instrumentality, and no amount of registration can mask that essential character. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you emphasize share capital, but does Article 12 require that the instrumentality be 'owned' by the State, or is 'control' the sole criterion? Take us to the authorities.
output.judge_notes: Strong start on the core issue; the 'name' argument is weak and could be attacked.

input.case_summary: Several petitioners challenged the validity of admissions to the Regional Engineering College, Srinagar, arguing that the college, registered as a society, was acting arbitrarily in its admission process. The petitioners contended that the college should be considered a 'State' under Article 12 of the Constitution and thus subject to fundamental rights. The Supreme Court held that the college was indeed a 'State' due to the deep and pervasive control of the Central and State governments, but ultimately dismissed the writ petitions, finding some aspects of the admission process arbitrary but declining to overturn the admissions due to the advanced stage of the academic year.
input.legal_issue: Whether the Regional Engineering College, Srinagar, registered as a society under the Jammu & Kashmir Registration of Societies Act, 1898, is a 'State' within the meaning of Article 12 of the Constitution of India and therefore subject to the fundamental rights guaranteed under Part III of the Constitution.
input.relevant_laws: Article 12 Constitution of India, Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 32 Constitution of India, Article 36 Constitution of India, Articles 309 Constitution of India, Articles 310 Constitution of India, Articles 311 Constitution of India, Jammu & Kashmir Registration of Societies Act, 1898, Societies Registration Act, 1860, Companies Act, 1956
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you emphasize share capital, but does Article 12 require that the instrumentality be 'owned' by the State, or is 'control' the sole criterion? Take us to the authorities.
input.current_argument: My Lords, in response to the question posed by the Hon'ble bench, the counsel humbly submits that Article 12 does not require ownership by the State as a necessary condition; rather, this Hon'ble Court in Sukhdev Singh v. Bhagatram and Ramana Dayaram Shetty v. International Airport Authority has held that 'control' is the decisive criterion, and share capital is merely one potent indicator of such control. The counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the fundamental distinction: ownership relates to legal title, while the concept of 'instrumentality or agency of the State' under Article 12 hinges on the degree of direct or indirect governmental control over the body's affairs. In the present case, the Regional Engineering College, though not owned, is under deep and pervasive control through governmental funding, appointment of governing body members, and regulatory oversight, which squarely satisfies the test enunciated by this Court. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the respondent on the same question.
output.judge_notes: Strong response — directly answered the question, used precise precedent, applied to facts.

input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the test for ‘State’ under Article 12 requires not mere financial involvement but such deep and pervasive control as to render the body a mere instrumentality of the Government. In Sabhajit Tewari v. Union of India, this Hon’ble Court held that a society registered under the Societies Registration Act does not become a ‘State’ absent total control over its affairs, distinguishing it from the statutory corporations in Sukhdev Singh. Applying that ratio to the present case, the Regional Engineering College, Srinagar, retains its independent character as a registered society under the Jammu & Kashmir Registration of Societies Act, with its own governing body and discretion in admissions—far from the degree of control that would convert it into an agency of the State. The counsel preemptively addresses the opposing argument: even assuming governmental funding and nominee appointments, the control here is not ‘deep and pervasive’ over the college’s day-to-day functioning, as required by this Court’s precedents. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel submits that the college is not amenable to Part III, and the petition must fail. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you rely on Sabhajit Tewari, but how do you reconcile that with this court's subsequent expansion of 'State' in Ramana Dayaram Shetty v. International Airport Authority of India?
output.judge_notes: Solid structure and correct citation of Sabhajit Tewari, but hasn't addressed post-Sukhdev evolution of the control test; needs to distinguish Ramana Dayaram Shetty.

input.case_summary: Several petitioners challenged the validity of admissions to the Regional Engineering College, Srinagar, arguing that the college, registered as a society, was acting arbitrarily in its admission process. The petitioners contended that the college should be considered a 'State' under Article 12 of the Constitution and thus subject to fundamental rights. The Supreme Court held that the college was indeed a 'State' due to the deep and pervasive control of the Central and State governments, but ultimately dismissed the writ petitions, finding some aspects of the admission process arbitrary but declining to overturn the admissions due to the advanced stage of the academic year.
input.legal_issue: Whether the Regional Engineering College, Srinagar, registered as a society under the Jammu & Kashmir Registration of Societies Act, 1898, is a 'State' within the meaning of Article 12 of the Constitution of India and therefore subject to the fundamental rights guaranteed under Part III of the Constitution.
input.relevant_laws: Article 12 Constitution of India, Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 32 Constitution of India, Article 36 Constitution of India, Articles 309 Constitution of India, Articles 310 Constitution of India, Articles 311 Constitution of India, Jammu & Kashmir Registration of Societies Act, 1898, Societies Registration Act, 1860, Companies Act, 1956
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you rely on Sabhajit Tewari, but how do you reconcile that with this court's subsequent expansion of 'State' in Ramana Dayaram Shetty v. International Airport Authority of India?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that Sabhajit Tewari remains fully binding and has never been overruled, as this court itself affirmed in DK Basu, Maneka Gandhi, Olga Tellis, and Vishaka. The expansion in Ramana Dayaram Shetty, while significant, must be read harmoniously with these settled precedents, as your Lordships did in countless subsequent decisions. If the ratio of Sabhajit Tewari were truly displaced, this court would have said so in those very cases—yet it did not. The counsel therefore respectfully submits that the college, as a registered society, falls squarely within the Sabhajit Tewari framework, and the petitioner's reliance on Ramana Dayaram Shetty is an attempt to stretch the law beyond what this court intended. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you say read harmoniously, but what specific test from Ramana Shetty can coexist with Sabhajit Tewari's approach? Please take us through the reconciling reasoning.
output.judge_notes: Cites multiple cases but doesn't explain how they affirm Sabhajit Tewari on Article 12 - needs to address Ramana Shetty's 'instrumentality' test head-on.

input.case_summary: Several petitioners challenged the validity of admissions to the Regional Engineering College, Srinagar, arguing that the college, registered as a society, was acting arbitrarily in its admission process. The petitioners contended that the college should be considered a 'State' under Article 12 of the Constitution and thus subject to fundamental rights. The Supreme Court held that the college was indeed a 'State' due to the deep and pervasive control of the Central and State governments, but ultimately dismissed the writ petitions, finding some aspects of the admission process arbitrary but declining to overturn the admissions due to the advanced stage of the academic year.
input.legal_issue: Whether the Regional Engineering College, Srinagar, registered as a society under the Jammu & Kashmir Registration of Societies Act, 1898, is a 'State' within the meaning of Article 12 of the Constitution of India and therefore subject to the fundamental rights guaranteed under Part III of the Constitution.
input.relevant_laws: Article 12 Constitution of India, Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 32 Constitution of India, Article 36 Constitution of India, Articles 309 Constitution of India, Articles 310 Constitution of India, Articles 311 Constitution of India, Jammu & Kashmir Registration of Societies Act, 1898, Societies Registration Act, 1860, Companies Act, 1956
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you say read harmoniously, but what specific test from Ramana Shetty can coexist with Sabhajit Tewari's approach? Please take us through the reconciling reasoning.
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the reconciling reasoning, as Your Lordships seek, must be drawn from the particular operational realities of this institution rather than abstract doctrinal alignment. The college, while receiving government funding, has consistently demonstrated a distinct character in its day-to-day functioning, including the design of its admission criteria and the composition of its interview panels. These factual aspects, My Lords, reveal a degree of autonomy that distinguishes this case from those where more pervasive control was exercised. Therefore, the two tests your Lordships mention can be harmonized by focusing on the actual extent of governmental intervention present here, which does not transform the college into a state instrumentality for the purposes of the constitutional framework. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel humbly rests this point.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the petitioner's response on the specific tests from Ramana Shetty that can coexist with Sabhajit Tewari.
output.judge_notes: Counsel failed to answer the question — avoided specific tests and gave generic operational autonomy argument. Needs to explicitly reconcile tests.

input.case_summary: Several petitioners challenged the validity of admissions to the Regional Engineering College, Srinagar, arguing that the college, registered as a society, was acting arbitrarily in its admission process. The petitioners contended that the college should be considered a 'State' under Article 12 of the Constitution and thus subject to fundamental rights. The Supreme Court held that the college was indeed a 'State' due to the deep and pervasive control of the Central and State governments, but ultimately dismissed the writ petitions, finding some aspects of the admission process arbitrary but declining to overturn the admissions due to the advanced stage of the academic year.
input.legal_issue: Whether the Regional Engineering College, Srinagar, registered as a society under the Jammu & Kashmir Registration of Societies Act, 1898, is a 'State' within the meaning of Article 12 of the Constitution of India and therefore subject to the fundamental rights guaranteed under Part III of the Constitution.
input.relevant_laws: Article 12 Constitution of India, Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 32 Constitution of India, Article 36 Constitution of India, Articles 309 Constitution of India, Articles 310 Constitution of India, Articles 311 Constitution of India, Jammu & Kashmir Registration of Societies Act, 1898, Societies Registration Act, 1860, Companies Act, 1956
input.opposing_last_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the reconciling reasoning, as Your Lordships seek, must be drawn from the particular operational realities of this institution rather than abstract doctrinal alignment. The college, while receiving government funding, has consistently demonstrated a distinct character in its day-to-day functioning, including the design of its admission criteria and the composition of its interview panels. These factual aspects, My Lords, reveal a degree of autonomy that distinguishes this case from those where more pervasive control was exercised. Therefore, the two tests your Lordships mention can be harmonized by focusing on the actual extent of governmental intervention present here, which does not transform the college into a state instrumentality for the purposes of the constitutional framework. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel humbly rests this point.
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that it is well settled in our constitutional law that the true test for determining whether a body is a 'State' under Article 12 is not the degree of autonomy it enjoys in its internal functioning, but the character and source of its creation, the nature of governmental control over its affairs, and the public purpose it serves. The law recognizes that when an entity is substantially funded and controlled by the government, and performs functions that are essentially governmental in nature, it cannot escape the discipline of fundamental rights merely by pointing to its operational independence in minor matters. The college, as the record shows, was established to fulfill a state policy of providing technical education, and its admissions process—being a gateway to public resources—must therefore conform to constitutional standards. My Lords, the suggestion that operational autonomy can overcome the structural reality of pervasive governmental control finds no support in the established framework of Article 12. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel humbly rests this point.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the respondent.
output.judge_notes: Strong rebuttal of autonomy argument; correctly applied structural control test but did not cite specific precedents.

input.case_summary: Several petitioners challenged the validity of admissions to the Regional Engineering College, Srinagar, arguing that the college, registered as a society, was acting arbitrarily in its admission process. The petitioners contended that the college should be considered a 'State' under Article 12 of the Constitution and thus subject to fundamental rights. The Supreme Court held that the college was indeed a 'State' due to the deep and pervasive control of the Central and State governments, but ultimately dismissed the writ petitions, finding some aspects of the admission process arbitrary but declining to overturn the admissions due to the advanced stage of the academic year.
input.legal_issue: Whether the Regional Engineering College, Srinagar, registered as a society under the Jammu & Kashmir Registration of Societies Act, 1898, is a 'State' within the meaning of Article 12 of the Constitution of India and therefore subject to the fundamental rights guaranteed under Part III of the Constitution.
input.relevant_laws: Article 12 Constitution of India, Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 32 Constitution of India, Article 36 Constitution of India, Articles 309 Constitution of India, Articles 310 Constitution of India, Articles 311 Constitution of India, Jammu & Kashmir Registration of Societies Act, 1898, Societies Registration Act, 1860, Companies Act, 1956
input.opposing_last_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that it is well settled in our constitutional law that the true test for determining whether a body is a 'State' under Article 12 is not the degree of autonomy it enjoys in its internal functioning, but the character and source of its creation, the nature of governmental control over its affairs, and the public purpose it serves. The law recognizes that when an entity is substantially funded and controlled by the government, and performs functions that are essentially governmental in nature, it cannot escape the discipline of fundamental rights merely by pointing to its operational independence in minor matters. The college, as the record shows, was established to fulfill a state policy of providing technical education, and its admissions process—being a gateway to public resources—must therefore conform to constitutional standards. My Lords, the suggestion that operational autonomy can overcome the structural reality of pervasive governmental control finds no support in the established framework of Article 12. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel humbly rests this point.
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the precedent in Sabhajit Tewary v. Union of India squarely governs this matter, where this Hon'ble Court held that a society registered under a general Societies Registration Act, even when substantially funded by the government, does not become a 'State' under Article 12 unless the government exercises such pervasive control as to reduce it to a mere departmental limb. My Lords, the counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the fact that the college here, like the Council of Scientific and Industrial Research in Sabhajit, is an autonomous body registered under the Jammu & Kashmir Registration of Societies Act, with its own governing council and rule-making power. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to proceed by submitting that the college's admission process is a matter of academic autonomy, not governmental action, and therefore falls outside the reach of Article 14. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the petitioner's rejoinder on the reliance on Sabhajit Tewary.
output.judge_notes: Relies heavily on Sabhajit which was overruled in this very case; weak on factual application to college's deep government control.

input.legal_issue: Whether the Regional Engineering College, Srinagar, registered as a society under the Jammu & Kashmir Registration of Societies Act, 1898, is a 'State' within the meaning of Article 12 of the Constitution of India and therefore subject to the fundamental rights guaranteed under Part III of the Constitution.
input.relevant_laws: Article 12 Constitution of India, Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 32 Constitution of India, Article 36 Constitution of India, Articles 309 Constitution of India, Articles 310 Constitution of India, Articles 311 Constitution of India, Jammu & Kashmir Registration of Societies Act, 1898, Societies Registration Act, 1860, Companies Act, 1956
input.opposing_last_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the precedent in Sabhajit Tewary v. My Lords, the counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the fact that the college here, like the Council of Scientific and Industrial Research in Sabhajit, is an autonomous body registered under the Jammu & Kashmir Registration of Societies Act, with its own governing council and rule-making power. output.judge_response: Counsel, you first argue the college is a State due to deep and pervasive control, then concede that the autonomy of its admission process shows it is not a departmental limb and thus fundamental rights do not apply — can you clarify your position on whether Article 12 covers the college or not?
output.judge_notes: Petitioner's argument is internally contradictory; claims deep and pervasive control but then uses autonomy to argue non-applicability of Part III.

input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the determination of whether the college is a 'State' under Article 12 is not strictly necessary for the relief sought in these proceedings. The counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the well-settled principle that even bodies not falling within Article 12 are amenable to writ jurisdiction under Article 226 if they perform public functions. output.judge_response: Counsel, if Article 226 provides a remedy, does that mean the college is not a 'State' and thus Article 14 does not bind it directly — how does that affect your challenge to the admission process?

input.case_summary: Brojo Nath Ganguly and Tarun Kanti Sengupta, employees of Central Inland Water Transport Corporation (a government company), were terminated under Rule 9(i) of the Corporation's service rules, which allowed termination with three months' notice. They challenged the termination and the validity of Rule 9(i) in the Calcutta High Court. The High Court ruled in their favor, holding the Corporation to be 'the State' under Article 12 and Rule 9(i) to be ultra vires Article 14. The Supreme Court upheld the High Court's decision, finding Rule 9(i) to be unconscionable and violative of Article 14.
input.legal_issue: Whether Rule 9(i) of the Central Inland Water Transport Corporation Limited Service, Discipline and Appeal Rules, 1979, allowing termination of permanent employees with three months' notice, is void as being opposed to public policy under Section 23 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 and as violating Article 14 of the Constitution of India.
input.relevant_laws: Article 12 Constitution of India, Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 38 Constitution of India, Article 39 Constitution of India, Article 226 Constitution of India, Article 298 Constitution of India, Section 16 Indian Contract Act, 1872, Section 19A Indian Contract Act, 1872, Section 23 Indian Contract Act, 1872, Section 617 Companies Act, 1956, Industrial Disputes Act, Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act, 1956
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that Rule 9(i) of the Corporation's service rules is a valid contractual term and cannot be struck down under Article 14. Following the ratio in Sabhajit Tewary v. Union of India, a government company is not 'the State' under Article 12, and therefore its employment contracts are not subject to constitutional limitations. The core issue is whether a private contract can be invalidated for unreasonableness under Article 14; the answer is no. Even if the counterargument invokes Section 23 of the Indian Contract Act, a termination clause with three months' notice is not opposed to public policy—it is a standard, reasonable provision agreed upon at the inception of employment. Hence, the High Court's decision must be reversed.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you rely on Sabhajit Tewary for the proposition that a government company is not State, but what is your response to the line of cases beginning with Sukhdev Singh and Ramana Dayaram Shetty that have expanded the definition of State under Article 12?
output.judge_notes: Relies on Sabhajit Tewary but does not address later expansive interpretations; public policy argument is underdeveloped.

input.relevant_laws: Article 12 Constitution of India, Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 38 Constitution of India, Article 39 Constitution of India, Article 226 Constitution of India, Article 298 Constitution of India, Section 16 Indian Contract Act, 1872, Section 19A Indian Contract Act, 1872, Section 23 Indian Contract Act, 1872, Section 617 Companies Act, 1956, Industrial Disputes Act, Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act, 1956
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you rely on Sabhajit Tewary for the proposition that a government company is not State, but what is your response to the line of cases beginning with Sukhdev Singh and Ramana Dayaram Shetty that have expanded the definition of State under Article 12?
input.current_argument: My Lords, in response to the question posed by the Hon'ble bench, the counsel humbly submits that the decisions in Sukhdev Singh and Ramana Dayaram Shetty are distinguishable as they involved entities exercising deep governmental control or performing functions of public importance, such as banking or public sector undertakings with monopolistic powers. In contrast, the Central Inland Water Transport Corporation is a purely commercial venture engaged in transport, not discharging any sovereign or public duty that would bring it within the expanded definition of 'State'. The counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the precise legal distinction that a government company merely operating a business does not become an instrumentality or agency of the State under Article 12, absent a functional nexus with governmental powers. Thus, Sabhajit Tewary remains applicable, and the Corporation's actions are not subject to the constraints of Article 14. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you distinguish on the basis of commercial function, but what if the Corporation is wholly owned and deeply controlled by the government – does that not bring it within the instrumentality test from Ramana Dayaram Shetty?
output.judge_notes: Distinguished Sukhdev Singh on function but did not address control test; may press further on deep government control.

input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you distinguish on the basis of commercial function, but what if the Corporation is wholly owned and deeply controlled by the government – does that not bring it within the instrumentality test from Ramana Dayaram Shetty?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that while the Corporation is indeed wholly owned and deeply controlled by the government, the instrumentality test from *Ramana Dayaram Shetty* does not mechanically apply to every commercial entity. The counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the fact that this Corporation performs no sovereign or public function—it operates purely on commercial lines, engaged in inland water transport like any private carrier. Mere ownership and control, without a public duty, cannot transform a trading company into 'the State' under Article 12; the opposing counsel's reliance on control alone conflates form with substance. Thus, even if control is deep, it does not satisfy the functional test required by this Court's precedents.

output.judge_response: Counsel, if the test in Ramana Shetty hinges on control and function, and the Corporation here is wholly owned and performs a commercial function similar to IAI, why should we not treat it as an instrumentality?
output.judge_notes: Counsel tries to distinguish on sovereign vs commercial but Ramana Shetty itself was a commercial airline — weak distinction.

input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the test in Ramana Shetty must be read harmoniously with the later pronouncements in Ajay Hasia, Pradeep Kumar Biswas, and Zee Telefilms, all of which consistently hold that commercial functions do not automatically convert a government company into the State. My Lords, the counsel draws the attention of the Hon’ble bench to the celebrated lines of authority in Maneka Gandhi, Olga Tellis, Vishaka, and DK Basu, which respectively underscore that the boundaries of Article 12 require strict construction when a body is engaged in purely economic activities. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to rely on the well-settled position that a corporation wholly owned by the Government but operating on commercial lines cannot be equated with a sovereign entity. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the respondent.
output.judge_notes: Counsel cited multiple cases but failed to explain why this corporation differs from those held to be instrumentalities.

input.case_summary: Brojo Nath Ganguly and Tarun Kanti Sengupta, employees of Central Inland Water Transport Corporation (a government company), were terminated under Rule 9(i) of the Corporation's service rules, which allowed termination with three months' notice. They challenged the termination and the validity of Rule 9(i) in the Calcutta High Court. The High Court ruled in their favor, holding the Corporation to be 'the State' under Article 12 and Rule 9(i) to be ultra vires Article 14. The Supreme Court upheld the High Court's decision, finding Rule 9(i) to be unconscionable and violative of Article 14.
input.legal_issue: Whether Rule 9(i) of the Central Inland Water Transport Corporation Limited Service, Discipline and Appeal Rules, 1979, allowing termination of permanent employees with three months' notice, is void as being opposed to public policy under Section 23 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 and as violating Article 14 of the Constitution of India.

Krishna Kant* (1995), where this Hon'ble Court held that a termination based on a contract provision is an industrial dispute, and the availability of a remedy before the industrial tribunal bars the writ jurisdiction. In that case, the rule in question was similarly challenged as arbitrary, yet the Court declined to entertain the writ because the industrial forum could examine all facets of the contract, including whether the term was void for being opposed to public policy. Here, the determination of public policy under Section 23 is a mixed question of law and fact requiring evidentiary appreciation, which the Tribunal is uniquely equipped to handle. Thus, the High Court ought not to have entertained the challenge under Article 226, and the petitioner submits that this appeal should be allowed on that ground alone. output.judge_notes: Counsel relies on Krishna Kant but that case dealt with a contractual termination, not a challenge to the rule itself as void ab initio under Section 23. Needs to address whether a void rule can be the basis of an industrial dispute.

input.case_summary: This case involves a writ petition filed in the Supreme Court of India after a review petition against a previous judgment was dismissed. The central issue is whether an aggrieved person can seek relief against a final judgment of the Supreme Court after their review petition has been rejected. The petitioners argued that there should be a mechanism to correct gross injustices even after review. The court considered whether Article 32 or inherent powers could be invoked. The Supreme Court ultimately held that while Article 32 cannot be used to challenge its own final judgments, a limited remedy might exist under its inherent powers to address gross abuse of process or miscarriage of justice, subject to specific procedural safeguards.
input.legal_issue: Whether an aggrieved person is entitled to any relief against a final judgment/order of the Supreme Court of India, after dismissal of a review petition, either under Article 32 of the Constitution or otherwise.
input.relevant_laws: Article 32 Constitution of India, Article 124 Constitution of India, Article 131 Constitution of India, Article 132 Constitution of India, Article 133 Constitution of India, Article 134 Constitution of India, Article 136 Constitution of India, Article 137 Constitution of India, Article 138 Constitution of India, Article 139 Constitution of India, Article 140 Constitution of India, Article 141 Constitution of India, Article 142 Constitution of India, Article 144 Constitution of India, Article 129 Constitution of India, Article 226 Constitution of India
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the present petition is fundamentally misconceived as it does not fall within the original jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court under Article 131 of the Constitution, which is strictly reserved for disputes between the Union and States or between States inter se. The matter at hand is a private matrimonial discord and thus lies entirely outside the constitutional framework governing such original suits. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to proceed by noting that the petitioner’s proper recourse was to a family court established under the Family Courts Act, 1984, and not to invoke the extraordinary writ jurisdiction of this Court. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you appear to be arguing against your own petition. Are you conceding that this Court has no jurisdiction to entertain the present matter?
output.judge_notes: Petitioner's counsel argued against own case, completely missing the issue of finality and review.

The petitioner's stand is that while Article 32 cannot lie against this Court's own final judgment after review, the Court retains its inherent jurisdiction under Article 142 and Article 129 to rectify a grave miscarriage of justice or jurisdictional error. output.judge_notes: Handled the pressure well by drawing a clear distinction between Article 32 and inherent powers, but the scope of Article 142 remains vague—needs to specify limits and safeguards.

Furthermore, the court may impose strict procedural conditions, such as a limited time for filing and a preliminary screening by a bench not involved in the original decision, to prevent abuse of process. input.case_summary: This case involves a writ petition filed in the Supreme Court of India after a review petition against a previous judgment was dismissed. input.legal_issue: Whether an aggrieved person is entitled to any relief against a final judgment/order of the Supreme Court of India, after dismissal of a review petition, either under Article 32 of the Constitution or otherwise.
input.relevant_laws: Article 32 Constitution of India, Article 124 Constitution of India, Article 131 Constitution of India, Article 132 Constitution of India, Article 133 Constitution of India, Article 134 Constitution of India, Article 136 Constitution of India, Article 137 Constitution of India, Article 138 Constitution of India, Article 139 Constitution of India, Article 140 Constitution of India, Article 141 Constitution of India, Article 142 Constitution of India, Article 144 Constitution of India, Article 129 Constitution of India, Article 226 Constitution of India

The ratio of this very case—Rupa Ashok Hurra itself—squarely holds that Article 32 cannot be invoked to assail a prior judgment, because a judicial order is not "state action" violative of fundamental rights in that context. My Lords, the principle of finality is constitutionally anchored in Article 141, which declares that the law stated by this Court is binding, and a contrary approach would convert Article 32 into an unlimited review, contradicting the specific remedy under Article 137. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel proceeds.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the petitioner's response to this point.
output.judge_notes: Distinguished three cited precedents clearly; directly answered the question with precise ratio of the case itself.
