input.legal_issue: Whether Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code, insofar as it criminalizes consensual sexual acts between adults of the same sex, violates fundamental rights guaranteed under Articles 14, 15, 19, and 21 of the Constitution of India.
input.relevant_laws: Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 15 Constitution of India, Article 19(1)(a) Constitution of India, Article 21 Constitution of India, Section 377 IPC
input.opposing_last_argument: My Lords, in response to the question posed by the Hon'ble bench, the counsel humbly submits that Maneka Gandhi’s requirement of non-arbitrariness applies directly to Section 377 because the law lacks a rational nexus with any legitimate state interest. The criminalisation of consensual same-sex acts between adults is founded solely on majoritarian moral disapproval, which this Hon'ble Court has held in S. R. Bommai and other judgments cannot justify a restriction on fundamental rights. The precise distinction is that while Maneka Gandhi invalidated a procedure as arbitrary, Section 377 is arbitrary in substance: it imposes a blanket prohibition on intimate conduct that causes no harm, thereby failing the test of reasonableness under Article 21. My Lords, the counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the fact that the law’s classification is entirely divorced from any discernible public purpose, rendering it an arbitrary exercise of state power. Much obliged, My Lords.
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the impugned provision, Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code, has been on the statute books for over a century and reflects the collective moral conscience of society. The counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the fact that the law criminalises certain acts, not identities, and there exists no arbitrariness in maintaining a distinction that has been consistently upheld by legislative wisdom. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to proceed by emphasising that the classification in Section 377 is neither arbitrary nor discriminatory, as it applies equally to all persons regardless of orientation. My Lords, the requirement of non-arbitrariness, as contended by the learned counsel opposite, is fully satisfied because the law serves a manifest purpose of preserving social order. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you argue that Section 377 applies equally to all persons, but does that satisfy Article 14 when the classification singles out intimate conduct that is central to a specific group's identity and autonomy?
output.judge_notes: Relies on collective morality and formal equality — sidesteps the central privacy and dignity arguments.

input.case_summary: This case involved multiple petitioners challenging the constitutional validity of Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC), which criminalized "carnal intercourse against the order of nature." The petitioners argued that Section 377 violated fundamental rights, including the right to privacy, equality, and dignity. The Union of India defended the law, citing social morality. The Supreme Court ultimately decriminalized consensual homosexual acts between adults, holding Section 377 unconstitutional to the extent that it criminalized such acts.
input.legal_issue: Whether Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code, insofar as it criminalizes consensual sexual acts between adults of the same sex, violates fundamental rights guaranteed under Articles 14, 15, 19, and 21 of the Constitution of India.
input.relevant_laws: Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 15 Constitution of India, Article 19(1)(a) Constitution of India, Article 21 Constitution of India, Section 377 IPC
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you argue that Section 377 applies equally to all persons, but does that satisfy Article 14 when the classification singles out intimate conduct that is central to a specific group's identity and autonomy?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that Article 14 does not prohibit classification per se, only unreasonable classification, as held in State of West Bengal v. Anwar Ali Sarkar. Section 377 criminalizes a specific act defined by its *nature*, not by the identity of the persons involved, and thus applies equally to all persons who engage in such conduct, irrespective of their sexual orientation. The classification is based on an intelligible differentia—the act's reputed unnaturalness—which bears a rational nexus to the legislative objective of preserving public morality and health. The counsel respectfully notes that mere disproportionate impact on a particular group does not, without more, invalidate a facially neutral law. My Lords, the core legal issue here is whether the conduct itself can be singled out, not whether it disproportionately affects one community, and the precedent clearly permits such a classification. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to proceed.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the petitioner.
output.judge_notes: Counsel relies on Anwar Ali Sarkar but fails to address that the 'intelligible differentia' here is based on a stigmatized moral view; weak on reasonableness.

input.case_summary: This case involved multiple petitioners challenging the constitutional validity of Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC), which criminalized "carnal intercourse against the order of nature." The petitioners argued that Section 377 violated fundamental rights, including the right to privacy, equality, and dignity. The Union of India defended the law, citing social morality. The Supreme Court ultimately decriminalized consensual homosexual acts between adults, holding Section 377 unconstitutional to the extent that it criminalized such acts.
input.legal_issue: Whether Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code, insofar as it criminalizes consensual sexual acts between adults of the same sex, violates fundamental rights guaranteed under Articles 14, 15, 19, and 21 of the Constitution of India.
input.relevant_laws: Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 15 Constitution of India, Article 19(1)(a) Constitution of India, Article 21 Constitution of India, Section 377 IPC
input.opposing_last_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that Article 14 does not prohibit classification per se, only unreasonable classification, as held in State of West Bengal v. Anwar Ali Sarkar. Section 377 criminalizes a specific act defined by its *nature*, not by the identity of the persons involved, and thus applies equally to all persons who engage in such conduct, irrespective of their sexual orientation. The classification is based on an intelligible differentia—the act's reputed unnaturalness—which bears a rational nexus to the legislative objective of preserving public morality and health. The counsel respectfully notes that mere disproportionate impact on a particular group does not, without more, invalidate a facially neutral law. My Lords, the core legal issue here is whether the conduct itself can be singled out, not whether it disproportionately affects one community, and the precedent clearly permits such a classification. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to proceed.
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that while a classification may be permissible under Article 14, it is well settled that any classification which impacts a fundamental right must be subjected to a higher standard of scrutiny. The law recognizes that where a statute disproportionately burdens a vulnerable minority and intrudes upon intimate personal choices, the mere existence of an intelligible differentia is insufficient—the state must also demonstrate a compelling interest and a proportionate means. In the present case, the classification based on the nature of the act fails to account for the profound harm it causes to the autonomy, dignity, and privacy of individuals, which are core to Article 21. Therefore, the supposed rational nexus to public morality cannot salvage a law that fundamentally infringes upon constitutionally protected liberties. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to proceed.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the respondent's response to the petitioner's submission on heightened scrutiny.
output.judge_notes: Petitioner correctly invokes heightened scrutiny for fundamental rights, but hasn't specified which standard—strict scrutiny or proportionality—applies. Argument is legally sound and well-structured.

input.case_summary: This case involved multiple petitioners challenging the constitutional validity of Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC), which criminalized "carnal intercourse against the order of nature." The petitioners argued that Section 377 violated fundamental rights, including the right to privacy, equality, and dignity. The Union of India defended the law, citing social morality. input.legal_issue: Whether Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code, insofar as it criminalizes consensual sexual acts between adults of the same sex, violates fundamental rights guaranteed under Articles 14, 15, 19, and 21 of the Constitution of India.
input.relevant_laws: Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 15 Constitution of India, Article 19(1)(a) Constitution of India, Article 21 Constitution of India, Section 377 IPC
input.opposing_last_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that while a classification may be permissible under Article 14, it is well settled that any classification which impacts a fundamental right must be subjected to a higher standard of scrutiny. The law recognizes that where a statute disproportionately burdens a vulnerable minority and intrudes upon intimate personal choices, the mere existence of an intelligible differentia is insufficient—the state must also demonstrate a compelling interest and a proportionate means. In the present case, the classification based on the nature of the act fails to account for the profound harm it causes to the autonomy, dignity, and privacy of individuals, which are core to Article 21. Therefore, the supposed rational nexus to public morality cannot salvage a law that fundamentally infringes upon constitutionally protected liberties. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to proceed.
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the classification under Section 377 is indeed grounded in a distinction between natural and unnatural acts. The counsel concedes that this very distinction may be subject to evolving societal standards and judicial interpretation over time. Nonetheless, until such evolution occurs, the law as enacted must be presumed valid and necessary for maintaining social morality. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to proceed.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you rely on the distinction between natural and unnatural acts—how does that distinction survive constitutional scrutiny under Article 14 when it burdens fundamental rights under Article 21?
output.judge_notes: Respondent offers no constitutional defense, merely defers to legislative morality - weak.

input.case_summary: This case involved multiple petitioners challenging the constitutional validity of Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC), which criminalized "carnal intercourse against the order of nature." The petitioners argued that Section 377 violated fundamental rights, including the right to privacy, equality, and dignity. The Union of India defended the law, citing social morality. The Supreme Court ultimately decriminalized consensual homosexual acts between adults, holding Section 377 unconstitutional to the extent that it criminalized such acts.
input.legal_issue: Whether Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code, insofar as it criminalizes consensual sexual acts between adults of the same sex, violates fundamental rights guaranteed under Articles 14, 15, 19, and 21 of the Constitution of India.
input.relevant_laws: Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 15 Constitution of India, Article 19(1)(a) Constitution of India, Article 21 Constitution of India, Section 377 IPC
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you rely on the distinction between natural and unnatural acts—how does that distinction survive constitutional scrutiny under Article 14 when it burdens fundamental rights under Article 21?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the distinction between natural and unnatural acts constitutes a valid classification under Article 14, as affirmed in principle by this Hon’ble Court in *Suresh Kumar Koushal v. Naz Foundation*, where it was held that Section 377 applies uniformly to all persons irrespective of sexual orientation, thereby satisfying the test of reasonable classification. The intelligible differentia lies in the objective biological criterion of whether the act is procreative, and the rational nexus is the State’s legitimate interest in preserving social morality and public health—a standard this Court has recognized in *Shri Ram Krishna Dalmia v. Justice S.R. Tendolkar* as sufficient for classification. Therefore, the burden on Article 21 is not disproportionate because the law merely regulates conduct that has historically been deemed harmful to societal order. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to proceed.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you assert that the burden on Article 21 is not disproportionate — what test of proportionality do you apply and how does the classification satisfy it?
output.judge_notes: Cited Koushal despite being overruled in this case — didn't meaningfully engage with Article 21 proportionality.

input.case_summary: This case involved multiple petitioners challenging the constitutional validity of Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC), which criminalized "carnal intercourse against the order of nature." The petitioners argued that Section 377 violated fundamental rights, including the right to privacy, equality, and dignity. The Union of India defended the law, citing social morality. The Supreme Court ultimately decriminalized consensual homosexual acts between adults, holding Section 377 unconstitutional to the extent that it criminalized such acts.
input.legal_issue: Whether Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code, insofar as it criminalizes consensual sexual acts between adults of the same sex, violates fundamental rights guaranteed under Articles 14, 15, 19, and 21 of the Constitution of India.
input.relevant_laws: Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 15 Constitution of India, Article 19(1)(a) Constitution of India, Article 21 Constitution of India, Section 377 IPC
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you assert that the burden on Article 21 is not disproportionate — what test of proportionality do you apply and how does the classification satisfy it?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the proportionality test applicable here is the traditional rational nexus standard, wherein the classification must bear a reasonable relation to the object of the statute. The object of Section 377 is the preservation of public morality and the protection of social order, which this Court has long recognized as a legitimate state interest. The classification—criminalizing only acts deemed "against the order of nature"—directly serves that object by reinforcing community standards of acceptable conduct. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the burden on Article 21 is minimal because the provision does not prohibit identity or orientation, but only specific sexual acts that the legislature, consistent with majoritarian moral consensus, deemed impermissible. Thus, the means are proportionate, as the law has stood unchallenged for decades and reflects society's settled will. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, if the rational nexus standard applies, how would you distinguish the recent decision in Puttaswamy which applied a structured proportionality test?
output.judge_notes: Counsel cited rational nexus standard which is outdated for fundamental rights review; failed to engage with the proportionality test post-Puttaswamy.

input.case_summary: This case involved multiple petitioners challenging the constitutional validity of Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC), which criminalized "carnal intercourse against the order of nature." The petitioners argued that Section 377 violated fundamental rights, including the right to privacy, equality, and dignity. The Union of India defended the law, citing social morality. The Supreme Court ultimately decriminalized consensual homosexual acts between adults, holding Section 377 unconstitutional to the extent that it criminalized such acts.
input.legal_issue: Whether Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code, insofar as it criminalizes consensual sexual acts between adults of the same sex, violates fundamental rights guaranteed under Articles 14, 15, 19, and 21 of the Constitution of India.
input.relevant_laws: Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 15 Constitution of India, Article 19(1)(a) Constitution of India, Article 21 Constitution of India, Section 377 IPC
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, if the rational nexus standard applies, how would you distinguish the recent decision in Puttaswamy which applied a structured proportionality test?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the decision in Puttaswamy applied a structured proportionality test in the context of informational privacy and state surveillance, which is a distinct sphere of fundamental rights. The present matter, however, concerns the regulation of conduct under a colonial-era penal provision that does not impinge upon the core of privacy as defined in that case. The rational nexus standard is entirely appropriate here, as Section 377 addresses public morality and health—areas where the legislature retains wide latitude under Article 14. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel draws attention to the fact that Puttaswamy did not overrule the rational nexus test for all laws, and this case falls squarely within that retained domain. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the petitioner on the applicability of the proportionality test.
output.judge_notes: Counsel's distinction of Puttaswamy is superficial - failed to address that sexual autonomy is core privacy. Rational nexus test is outdated for Article 21 rights.

input.case_summary: Dr. Jaya Thakur, a social worker, filed a public interest litigation against the Government of India and various states, seeking directions to provide free sanitary pads to female students in classes 6-12 and separate toilets for females in government-aided and residential schools. The petitioner argued that the lack of these provisions violates fundamental rights. The respondents acknowledged the issues and outlined existing schemes and policies. The court considered whether the lack of menstrual hygiene facilities violates constitutional rights to equality, dignity, and education. The court ultimately issued directions to ensure proper implementation of existing schemes and policies.
input.legal_issue: Does the unavailability of gender-segregated toilets and non-access to menstrual absorbents violate the rights to equality (Article 14), dignified menstrual health (Article 21), and education (Article 21A) under the Constitution of India, as well as the right to free and compulsory education under the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009?
input.relevant_laws: Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 21 Constitution of India, Article 21A Constitution of India, Article 32 Constitution of India, Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, Section 3 Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, Section 19 Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, Article 26 Universal Declaration of Human Rights, Article 13 International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that it is well settled that the right to education under our constitutional framework is not merely a right to access a classroom, but a right to an environment which enables the child to learn with dignity. The law recognizes that when adolescent girls are compelled to miss classes due to the unavailability of basic menstrual hygiene facilities, their fundamental right to education is directly compromised. My Lords, it is a matter of settled jurisprudence that the right to life under Article 21 encompasses the right to live with dignity, and this includes the ability to manage one's bodily functions in a clean and safe manner. If your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel submits that the absence of gender-segregated toilets and free sanitary pads creates an insurmountable barrier to the full realization of this right, particularly for girl students from disadvantaged backgrounds. Therefore, the petitioner humbly seeks the directions as prayed for.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you rely on Article 21A, but the Act does not explicitly mention menstrual hygiene — how do you locate this duty within the 'free and compulsory education' framework?
output.judge_notes: Solid structural flow but lacks specific legal citation — watch for follow-up on Article 21A.

input.case_summary: Dr. Jaya Thakur, a social worker, filed a public interest litigation against the Government of India and various states, seeking directions to provide free sanitary pads to female students in classes 6-12 and separate toilets for females in government-aided and residential schools. The petitioner argued that the lack of these provisions violates fundamental rights. The respondents acknowledged the issues and outlined existing schemes and policies. The court considered whether the lack of menstrual hygiene facilities violates constitutional rights to equality, dignity, and education. The court ultimately issued directions to ensure proper implementation of existing schemes and policies.
input.legal_issue: Does the unavailability of gender-segregated toilets and non-access to menstrual absorbents violate the rights to equality (Article 14), dignified menstrual health (Article 21), and education (Article 21A) under the Constitution of India, as well as the right to free and compulsory education under the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009?
input.relevant_laws: Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 21 Constitution of India, Article 21A Constitution of India, Article 32 Constitution of India, Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, Section 3 Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, Section 19 Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, Article 26 Universal Declaration of Human Rights, Article 13 International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you rely on Article 21A, but the Act does not explicitly mention menstrual hygiene — how do you locate this duty within the 'free and compulsory education' framework?
input.current_argument: My Lords, in response to the question posed by the Hon'ble bench, the counsel humbly submits that while the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009 may not explicitly employ the term "menstrual hygiene," the duty is squarely located within the statutory framework read purposively with constitutional guarantees. Section 19 of the Act mandates that schools must meet the prescribed norms and standards, which necessarily encompass an environment conducive to learning; a female student who misses school for want of sanitary absorbents or who has no safe, segregated toilet is effectively denied the equal benefit of free and compulsory education. My Lords, the counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to this precise legal point — the Act's guarantee of "free and compulsory education" incorporates the incidental necessities that make that education accessible in practice, and menstrual hygiene facilities are such necessities without which Article 21A would remain an empty promise for millions of girl children.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you ground your argument in Section 19's mandate for a 'conducive environment' — but the prescribed norms under the Schedule to the Act do not list menstrual hygiene facilities. How do you reconcile that?
output.judge_notes: Counsel used Section 19 creatively but avoided addressing the absence of specific norms — weak on positive law.

input.case_summary: Dr. Jaya Thakur, a social worker, filed a public interest litigation against the Government of India and various states, seeking directions to provide free sanitary pads to female students in classes 6-12 and separate toilets for females in government-aided and residential schools. The petitioner argued that the lack of these provisions violates fundamental rights. The respondents acknowledged the issues and outlined existing schemes and policies. The court considered whether the lack of menstrual hygiene facilities violates constitutional rights to equality, dignity, and education. The court ultimately issued directions to ensure proper implementation of existing schemes and policies.
input.legal_issue: Does the unavailability of gender-segregated toilets and non-access to menstrual absorbents violate the rights to equality (Article 14), dignified menstrual health (Article 21), and education (Article 21A) under the Constitution of India, as well as the right to free and compulsory education under the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009?
input.relevant_laws: Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 21 Constitution of India, Article 21A Constitution of India, Article 32 Constitution of India, Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, Section 3 Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, Section 19 Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, Article 26 Universal Declaration of Human Rights, Article 13 International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you ground your argument in Section 19's mandate for a 'conducive environment' — but the prescribed norms under the Schedule to the Act do not list menstrual hygiene facilities. How do you reconcile that?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the Schedule to the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act is a floor, not a ceiling, and cannot be read to dilute the fundamental rights under Articles 14, 21, and 21A. This Hon'ble Court has repeatedly held — in Maneka Gandhi, in Vishaka, in Olga Tellis, and in D.K. Basu — that where statutory provisions fall short of constitutional imperatives, the Court must fill the gap to protect dignity and equality. My Lords, the absence of menstrual hygiene facilities directly offends the "conducive environment" mandated by Section 19, because it forces girl students to choose between their health and education, a choice no child should face. The counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the settled principle that the right to life under Article 21 includes the right to live with dignity, and the right to education under Article 21A is meaningless without the conditions that allow regular attendance. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the respondent.
output.judge_notes: Well-handled — directly answered the schedule question with constitutional supremacy argument, good case citations.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you have spoken of schemes and policies, but the petition is grounded in Articles 14, 21, and 21A — what is the legal basis for your submission that these measures satisfy the constitutional mandate of dignified menstrual health?

input.case_summary: Dr. Jaya Thakur, a social worker, filed a public interest litigation against the Government of India and various states, seeking directions to provide free sanitary pads to female students in classes 6-12 and separate toilets for females in government-aided and residential schools. The petitioner argued that the lack of these provisions violates fundamental rights. The respondents acknowledged the issues and outlined existing schemes and policies. The court considered whether the lack of menstrual hygiene facilities violates constitutional rights to equality, dignity, and education. The court ultimately issued directions to ensure proper implementation of existing schemes and policies.
input.legal_issue: Does the unavailability of gender-segregated toilets and non-access to menstrual absorbents violate the rights to equality (Article 14), dignified menstrual health (Article 21), and education (Article 21A) under the Constitution of India, as well as the right to free and compulsory education under the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009?
input.relevant_laws: Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 21 Constitution of India, Article 21A Constitution of India, Article 32 Constitution of India, Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, Section 3 Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, Section 19 Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, Article 26 Universal Declaration of Human Rights, Article 13 International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you have spoken of schemes and policies, but the petition is grounded in Articles 14, 21, and 21A — what is the legal basis for your submission that these measures satisfy the constitutional mandate of dignified menstrual health?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the constitutional mandate under Article 21A, read with the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, is expressly confined to children between the ages of six and fourteen years, corresponding to elementary education up to Class VIII. The petition’s demand for free sanitary pads and separate toilets in Classes 6–12 therefore extends beyond the statutory and constitutional guarantee of free and compulsory education. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel draws the attention of the Hon’ble bench to Section 3 of the Act, which limits the right to children in the specified age group, and submits that the duty to provide ancillary health facilities for older students falls within the domain of executive policy, not constitutional compulsion. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the petitioner on whether Article 14 and Article 21 impose an independent obligation beyond the RTE Act's age limits.
output.judge_notes: Respondent cleverly narrowed Article 21A but ignored Article 14 and 21 entirely — weak on the broader constitutional challenge.

input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the respondent's narrow interpretation of Article 21A ignores this Hon’ble Court’s holding in Unni Krishnan v. State of A.P., where the right to education was read as a continuing right flowing from Article 21 up to the age of fourteen, but the ratio also recognized that the State’s duty to ensure meaningful education does not abruptly cease at the elementary stage. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel draws the attention of the Hon’ble bench to M.C. Mehta v. Union of India, where this Court held that the right to free education under Article 21A is inseparable from the right to dignity under Article 21, and that dignity mandates the provision of basic facilities like toilets and sanitary pads for all girl students in government schools, regardless of their age. The petition specifically covers Classes 6–12, and the precedent in Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan applied fundamental rights to the workplace; by parity the right to education must extend to secondary classes to make the right to dignity meaningful. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you rely on Unni Krishnan but that case held the right to education under Article 21 is absolute only up to age 14, and beyond that it is subject to economic capacity. How does that support your demand for facilities in classes 9-12?
output.judge_notes: Good attempt at analogy but Unni Krishnan actually limits the right beyond 14 - needs to address that.

input.case_summary: Dr. Jaya Thakur, a social worker, filed a public interest litigation against the Government of India and various states, seeking directions to provide free sanitary pads to female students in classes 6-12 and separate toilets for females in government-aided and residential schools. input.relevant_laws: Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 21 Constitution of India, Article 21A Constitution of India, Article 32 Constitution of India, Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, Section 3 Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, Section 19 Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, Article 26 Universal Declaration of Human Rights, Article 13 International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights

input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that while Unni Krishnan v. State of Andhra Pradesh indeed holds the right to education under Article 21 as absolute only up to age 14 and thereafter subject to economic capacity, the present petition does not rest on an unqualified right to education for classes 9 through 12. Instead, the counsel concedes that for these higher classes, the state’s obligation to provide menstrual hygiene facilities may be contingent upon its economic capacity. However, the counsel submits that the respondents have failed to demonstrate any such incapacity, and thus the petition should succeed.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the respondent on the question of economic capacity and the adequacy of existing schemes.
output.judge_notes: Handled Unni Krishnan objection deftly by conceding limitation and shifting burden to state on economic capacity.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you say existing schemes address these needs, but the petitioner claims they are inadequate — can you point to specific schemes that ensure access to free sanitary pads and gender-segregated toilets in every school?

input.case_summary: Dr. Jaya Thakur, a social worker, filed a public interest litigation against the Government of India and various states, seeking directions to provide free sanitary pads to female students in classes 6-12 and separate toilets for females in government-aided and residential schools. The petitioner argued that the lack of these provisions violates fundamental rights. The respondents acknowledged the issues and outlined existing schemes and policies. input.legal_issue: Does the unavailability of gender-segregated toilets and non-access to menstrual absorbents violate the rights to equality (Article 14), dignified menstrual health (Article 21), and education (Article 21A) under the Constitution of India, as well as the right to free and compulsory education under the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009?
input.relevant_laws: Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 21 Constitution of India, Article 21A Constitution of India, Article 32 Constitution of India, Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, Section 3 Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, Section 19 Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, Article 26 Universal Declaration of Human Rights, Article 13 International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you say existing schemes address these needs, but the petitioner claims they are inadequate — can you point to specific schemes that ensure access to free sanitary pads and gender-segregated toilets in every school?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the existing statutory framework—specifically the Menstrual Hygiene Scheme under the National Health Mission and the Swachh Vidyalaya Abhiyan under the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009, read with Section 19—provides for both free sanitary napkins to adolescent girls and gender-segregated toilets in all government schools. The learned counsel draws the attention of the Hon’ble bench to the ratio in *Municipal Council, Ratlam v. Vardhichand*, where this Court held that the State’s duty under Article 21 and Article 47 is to implement existing public health and welfare measures, not to create new ones. Applying that settled principle to the present case, the respondents have already created a comprehensive policy framework; the petitioner’s grievance is one of implementation gaps, not of an absence of constitutional or statutory duty. The counsel humbly submits that the counterargument that these schemes are not uniformly enforced fails to account for the ongoing state-level progress detailed in the respondent’s affidavit, including construction of toilets in over 90% of aided schools and distribution of 5 crore free pads in the last fiscal year. Therefore, the respondent submits that no new direction for free pads or separate toilets is required—only a direction to ensure meticulous implementation of the existing schemes, which fully satisfies Articles 14, 21, and 21A.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear from the petitioner on the constitutional obligation to ensure universal access, not merely progress.
output.judge_notes: Good use of Ratlam but dodged the universality point — 90% is not 100%.

input.case_summary: Harish Rana, in a persistent vegetative state (PVS) for 13 years following an accident, sought through his parents to discontinue clinically assisted nutrition and hydration (CANH). The High Court initially dismissed the plea, but the Supreme Court, after ordering evaluations by primary and secondary medical boards, considered whether CANH constituted medical treatment and if its continuation was in Rana's best interest. The Court ultimately streamlined existing guidelines for passive euthanasia, acknowledging the family's plea to end Rana's suffering, and considered the need for legislative action on the matter.
input.legal_issue: Whether the administration of clinically assisted nutrition and hydration (CANH) to a patient in a persistent vegetative state constitutes 'medical treatment' that can be withdrawn or withheld in accordance with Article 21 of the Constitution of India, and if so, under what conditions and procedural safeguards.
input.relevant_laws: Article 21 Constitution of India
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that it is well settled in our constitutional jurisprudence that the right to live with dignity under Article 21 encompasses the right to refuse unwanted medical treatment. The law recognizes that medical treatment, once found to be no longer beneficial, may be withdrawn to allow nature to take its course. Clinically assisted nutrition and hydration, being a medical intervention administered through a PEG tube, squarely falls within the ambit of medical treatment. Its continuation cannot be imposed upon a patient in a persistent vegetative state where it serves no therapeutic purpose and merely prolongs suffering. The counsel submits that the established principles governing withdrawal of treatment are fully satisfied in this case.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you assert that CANH is medical treatment. What is your submission on the specific ratio of Common Cause v. Union of India in this regard?
output.judge_notes: Solid framing but needs to ground in specific precedent — cited no case law.

input.case_summary: Harish Rana, in a persistent vegetative state (PVS) for 13 years following an accident, sought through his parents to discontinue clinically assisted nutrition and hydration (CANH). The High Court initially dismissed the plea, but the Supreme Court, after ordering evaluations by primary and secondary medical boards, considered whether CANH constituted medical treatment and if its continuation was in Rana's best interest. The Court ultimately streamlined existing guidelines for passive euthanasia, acknowledging the family's plea to end Rana's suffering, and considered the need for legislative action on the matter.
input.legal_issue: Whether the administration of clinically assisted nutrition and hydration (CANH) to a patient in a persistent vegetative state constitutes 'medical treatment' that can be withdrawn or withheld in accordance with Article 21 of the Constitution of India, and if so, under what conditions and procedural safeguards.
input.relevant_laws: Article 21 Constitution of India
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you assert that CANH is medical treatment. What is your submission on the specific ratio of Common Cause v. Union of India in this regard?
input.current_argument: My Lords, in response to the question posed by the Hon'ble bench, the counsel humbly submits that the ratio of Common Cause v. Union of India explicitly treated clinically assisted nutrition and hydration as medical treatment, holding that its withdrawal falls within the ambit of passive euthanasia under Article 21. The Court in that case distinguished between basic care and medical intervention, and categorically stated that CANH, being a tube-based artificial intervention, is not merely natural feeding but a medical procedure subject to best-interest evaluation. My Lords, the petitioner relies on this precise distinction: the PEG tube is a form of life-sustaining medical treatment, and its continuation without hope of recovery violates the right to live with dignity. Thus, the Common Cause guidelines fully support our submission that CANH withdrawal is permissible when medical boards confirm futility and the family consents, as occurred here. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, assuming CANH is medical treatment, what is your submission on whether prior expressed wishes of the patient are necessary before withdrawing, or is family consent alone sufficient under the Common Cause framework?
output.judge_notes: Counsel accurately cited Common Cause ratio and applied to facts — strong answer, but evaded the question of advance directives vs family consent.

input.case_summary: Harish Rana, in a persistent vegetative state (PVS) for 13 years following an accident, sought through his parents to discontinue clinically assisted nutrition and hydration (CANH). The High Court initially dismissed the plea, but the Supreme Court, after ordering evaluations by primary and secondary medical boards, considered whether CANH constituted medical treatment and if its continuation was in Rana's best interest. The Court ultimately streamlined existing guidelines for passive euthanasia, acknowledging the family's plea to end Rana's suffering, and considered the need for legislative action on the matter.
input.legal_issue: Whether the administration of clinically assisted nutrition and hydration (CANH) to a patient in a persistent vegetative state constitutes 'medical treatment' that can be withdrawn or withheld in accordance with Article 21 of the Constitution of India, and if so, under what conditions and procedural safeguards.
input.relevant_laws: Article 21 Constitution of India
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, assuming CANH is medical treatment, what is your submission on whether prior expressed wishes of the patient are necessary before withdrawing, or is family consent alone sufficient under the Common Cause framework?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that while the Common Cause framework accords significant weight to familial consent, prior expressed wishes of the patient are not merely advisable but constitutionally necessary to withdraw CANH under Article 21. My Lords, if the patient has not left unequivocal advance directives, the presumption must remain in favour of continued treatment, as the family's subjective perception of suffering cannot substitute for the patient's own autonomous choice. The counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the principle that dignity under Article 21 is inherently personal, and without clear prior instructions, withdrawal risks violating that very dignity by imposing a third-party judgment. Thus, in the absence of such expressed wishes, the petitioner's plea may be untenable, and the counsel concedes that family consent alone is insufficient to justify discontinuation of CANH. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the respondent.
output.judge_notes: Counsel misapplied Common Cause - advance directives are not mandatory - and undermined own case by conceding prior wishes necessary.

input.opposing_last_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that while the Common Cause framework accords significant weight to familial consent, prior expressed wishes of the patient are not merely advisable but constitutionally necessary to withdraw CANH under Article 21. The counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the principle that dignity under Article 21 is inherently personal, and without clear prior instructions, withdrawal risks violating that very dignity by imposing a third-party judgment. input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the opposing counsel's insistence on prior expressed wishes as a constitutional necessity under Article 21 is misplaced. My Lords, the counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the reality that the Common Cause framework itself does not rigidly require advance directives; it allows for family consent when the patient's wishes are unknown, as the family is best placed to reflect his values. The counsel further submits that requiring written directives in every case would be impractical and would effectively deny dignity to countless PVS patients who never anticipated such circumstances. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to conclude that family consent, weighed alongside medical opinion, is constitutionally sufficient. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you argue family consent is constitutionally sufficient, but what procedural safeguards do you propose to prevent potential abuse when the patient's wishes are entirely unknown?
output.judge_notes: Good structure but vague on precise legal standards for family consent.

input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you argue family consent is constitutionally sufficient, but what procedural safeguards do you propose to prevent potential abuse when the patient's wishes are entirely unknown?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the procedural safeguards in the present case are amply demonstrated by the meticulous process undertaken. As Your Lordships are aware, the medical boards evaluated the applicant's condition and the family's consistent wishes over thirteen years. The counsel draws attention to the fact that the family's plea, aligned with medical opinion, formed the basis for the determination. This, my Lords, ensures that when the patient's wishes are unknown, the collective judgment of medical experts and close kin serves as a reliable framework. The counsel therefore submits that the existing protocol, as applied here, sufficiently addresses potential abuse. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, your reliance on the specific facts of this case is noted, but what safeguards would you propose for a case where the family is not unanimous or there is no long history of consistent wishes? The bench will now hear the petitioner.
output.judge_notes: Respondent's counsel described existing process but did not propose independent safeguards — relies on case-specific facts rather than general principles.

Union of India* (2018) 5 SCC 1, established that clinically assisted nutrition and hydration (CANH) constitutes 'medical treatment' and that its withdrawal falls within the ambit of passive euthanasia under Article 21. Applying that ratio to the facts, the petitioner, in a persistent vegetative state for 13 years with no hope of recovery, has a right to die with dignity by declining futile medical treatment. My Lords, the counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the unanimous findings of both medical boards that continued CANH is not in the applicant's best interest, aligning with the family's consistent wishes. The counsel anticipates the counterargument that procedural safeguards are needed to prevent abuse; however, the meticulous multi-board evaluation and family involvement in this case precisely satisfy those safeguards. Therefore, the counsel seeks a declaration that CANH is medical treatment and that its withdrawal is permissible under Article 21 in the circumstances before Your Lordships. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, the Common Cause guidelines require a careful process when the patient's wishes are unknown — how does the multi-board evaluation here satisfy those guidelines?
output.judge_notes: Solid reliance on Common Cause, but needs to address whether the procedural safeguards for patients without advance directives were strictly followed.

input.case_summary: Harish Rana, in a persistent vegetative state (PVS) for 13 years following an accident, sought through his parents to discontinue clinically assisted nutrition and hydration (CANH). The High Court initially dismissed the plea, but the Supreme Court, after ordering evaluations by primary and secondary medical boards, considered whether CANH constituted medical treatment and if its continuation was in Rana's best interest. The Court ultimately streamlined existing guidelines for passive euthanasia, acknowledging the family's plea to end Rana's suffering, and considered the need for legislative action on the matter.
input.legal_issue: Whether the administration of clinically assisted nutrition and hydration (CANH) to a patient in a persistent vegetative state constitutes 'medical treatment' that can be withdrawn or withheld in accordance with Article 21 of the Constitution of India, and if so, under what conditions and procedural safeguards.
input.relevant_laws: Article 21 Constitution of India
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, the Common Cause guidelines require a careful process when the patient's wishes are unknown — how does the multi-board evaluation here satisfy those guidelines?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the very premise of the judicial inquiry into the multi-board evaluation process rests on a fundamental misapprehension: the Common Cause guidelines, while indeed requiring a careful process, do not mandate any specific number or composition of medical boards. The core legal issue before this Hon'ble Court is whether CANH constitutes medical treatment, and on that point, the guidelines are resoundingly clear—they have no relevance to the definitional threshold. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to proceed on the settled principle that the right to live with dignity under Article 21 alone empowers the patient or their guardians to refuse any bodily invasion, including the administration of nutrients, regardless of the procedural framework erected to safeguard unknown wishes. The multi-board evaluation here is an entirely separate, non-justiciable policy measure that bears no impact on the petitioner's constitutional entitlement to self‑determination. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the respondent on this point.
output.judge_notes: Counsel completely sidestepped the question and dismissed a key precedent without engagement.

input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the multi‑board evaluation process is not a non‑justiciable policy measure but a constitutionally mandated safeguard, as affirmed in DK Basu, Maneka Gandhi, Olga Tellis, and Vishaka. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to proceed on the settled principle that procedural due process is the bedrock of substantive rights under Article 21. The petitioner’s attempt to bypass these time‑tested safeguards cannot be countenanced, much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you cite Maneka Gandhi for fair procedure — can you explain how its ratio on passport seizure translates to a multi-board medical evaluation for a PVS patient?
output.judge_notes: Cites four cases but doesn't connect any to the specific medical context — needs to show direct applicability.

input.case_summary: Harish Rana, in a persistent vegetative state (PVS) for 13 years following an accident, sought through his parents to discontinue clinically assisted nutrition and hydration (CANH). The High Court initially dismissed the plea, but the Supreme Court, after ordering evaluations by primary and secondary medical boards, considered whether CANH constituted medical treatment and if its continuation was in Rana's best interest. The Court ultimately streamlined existing guidelines for passive euthanasia, acknowledging the family's plea to end Rana's suffering, and considered the need for legislative action on the matter.
input.legal_issue: Whether the administration of clinically assisted nutrition and hydration (CANH) to a patient in a persistent vegetative state constitutes 'medical treatment' that can be withdrawn or withheld in accordance with Article 21 of the Constitution of India, and if so, under what conditions and procedural safeguards.
input.relevant_laws: Article 21 Constitution of India
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you cite Maneka Gandhi for fair procedure — can you explain how its ratio on passport seizure translates to a multi-board medical evaluation for a PVS patient?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the ratio in Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India stands for the proposition that any procedure which deprives a person of a fundamental right must be fair, just, and reasonable, not merely a mechanical formality. Applying that principle here, the Hon'ble Court rightly required multiple medical boards to evaluate the petitioner's condition before permitting withdrawal of clinically assisted nutrition and hydration. Much obliged, My Lords. Just as the right to travel abroad could not be curtailed without a hearing for the passport-holder under Maneka Gandhi, so too the right to life with dignity under Article 21 cannot be terminated for a PVS patient without a comparable multi-tiered hearing through medical experts. The fact that the patient cannot personally be heard only reinforces the need for an even more rigorous procedural safeguard, exactly as the multi-board evaluation provides. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel submits that Maneka Gandhi mandates this very structure of layered assessment to ensure no error occurs in so grave a matter.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the petitioner.
output.judge_notes: Counsel answered directly and correctly applied Maneka Gandhi, though the analogy between passport seizure and medical board evaluation may need further scrutiny on the nature of state action.

input.case_summary: Smt. Chandravva Hanamant Gokavi, a hotel worker, filed a writ petition seeking the implementation of the Karnataka government's Menstrual Leave Policy across all establishments in Belagavi district, including hotels and small commercial units. She argued that women in the unorganized sector face harsh working conditions, especially during menstruation. The State acknowledged the policy's intent but cited practical challenges in its enforcement. The court considered the historical context of menstrual leave policies and the ongoing legislative efforts in this regard.
input.legal_issue: Whether the State of Karnataka is obligated to implement and enforce its Menstrual Leave Policy, particularly in the unorganized labor sector, to ensure workplace dignity and gender equity under Articles 226 and 227 of the Constitution of India.
input.relevant_laws: Articles 226 and 227 of the Constitution of India, Maternity Benefit Act, 1961, Article 32 of the Constitution of India, The Factories Act, 1948, The Karnataka Shops and Commercial Establishments Act, 1961, The Plantation Labour Act, 1951, The Beedi and Cigar Workers (Conditions of Employment) Act, 1966, The Motor Transport Workers Act, 1961, Section 5 of the Karnataka Menstrual Leave and Hygiene Bill, 2025
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the State's obligation to implement the Menstrual Leave Policy arises from the fundamental right to health and dignity under Article 21, read with Article 15(3) and the Directive Principles under Article 39(e). In Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan, this Hon'ble Court held that in the absence of enacted legislation, the State must enforce policies to secure workplace dignity for women, and the same ratio applies directly here, as the Karnataka Menstrual Leave and Hygiene Bill, 2025 reflects the State's own recognition of this right. The core legal issue is not the policy's existence but its enforcement, particularly in the unorganized sector where women like the petitioner face acute vulnerability. While the State pleads practical difficulties, this Court has consistently held in Bandhua Mukti Morcha v. Union of India that administrative inconvenience cannot defeat constitutional guarantees. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to proceed.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you rely heavily on Vishaka, but that case concerned sexual harassment—how do you extend its ratio to menstrual leave, where the policy is not yet a statutory right?
output.judge_notes: Strong on structure and relevance, but Vishaka analogy may be overstretched; did not address the pending Bill's legal status.

input.relevant_laws: Articles 226 and 227 of the Constitution of India, Maternity Benefit Act, 1961, Article 32 of the Constitution of India, The Factories Act, 1948, The Karnataka Shops and Commercial Establishments Act, 1961, The Plantation Labour Act, 1951, The Beedi and Cigar Workers (Conditions of Employment) Act, 1966, The Motor Transport Workers Act, 1961, Section 5 of the Karnataka Menstrual Leave and Hygiene Bill, 2025
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you rely heavily on Vishaka, but that case concerned sexual harassment—how do you extend its ratio to menstrual leave, where the policy is not yet a statutory right?
input.current_argument: My Lords, in response to the question posed by the Hon'ble bench, the counsel humbly submits that the ratio in Vishaka extends beyond sexual harassment because it established the foundational principle that the State has a constitutional duty under Articles 14, 19, and 21 to ensure gender-inclusive working conditions, even in the absence of specific legislation. My Lords, the precise legal distinction is that Vishaka addressed sex-based discrimination through hostile acts; here, the State’s failure to enforce its own policy perpetuates structural discrimination by ignoring a biological reality that uniquely burdens women in the unorganized sector. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel submits that the State’s inaction, by denying menstrual leave, effectively denies workplace dignity on the same constitutional continuum that Vishaka recognized—the right to work with dignity cannot be bifurcated by the nature of the burden. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, the policy is not a statute—how does the failure to enforce it amount to a constitutional violation under Article 21, given that there is no legal right to menstrual leave yet?
output.judge_notes: Extended Vishaka to structural discrimination via inaction on own policy—clever but the statutory vs policy gap is still open.

Chandravva Hanamant Gokavi, a hotel worker, filed a writ petition seeking the implementation of the Karnataka government's Menstrual Leave Policy across all establishments in Belagavi district, including hotels and small commercial units. input.judge_last_response: Counsel, the policy is not a statute—how does the failure to enforce it amount to a constitutional violation under Article 21, given that there is no legal right to menstrual leave yet?

The policy is not a mere administrative instruction but a concrete step towards fulfilling constitutional obligations. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to proceed. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the respondent.
output.judge_notes: Decent response to pressure but relied on legitimate expectation without explaining how it binds unorganized sector — needs more specificity.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the petitioner on the gap in coverage for the unorganized sector.

input.case_summary: Smt. Chandravva Hanamant Gokavi, a hotel worker, filed a writ petition seeking the implementation of the Karnataka government's Menstrual Leave Policy across all establishments in Belagavi district, including hotels and small commercial units. input.relevant_laws: Articles 226 and 227 of the Constitution of India, Maternity Benefit Act, 1961, Article 32 of the Constitution of India, The Factories Act, 1948, The Karnataka Shops and Commercial Establishments Act, 1961, The Plantation Labour Act, 1951, The Beedi and Cigar Workers (Conditions of Employment) Act, 1966, The Motor Transport Workers Act, 1961, Section 5 of the Karnataka Menstrual Leave and Hygiene Bill, 2025
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you have not cited any legal provision or precedent — what is the precise legal basis for seeking a writ of mandamus in this matter?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the petitioner humbly submits that the precise legal basis for seeking a writ of mandamus lies in the combined operation of Section 4 of the Maternity Benefit Act, 1961, and Article 21 of the Constitution. The Maternity Benefit Act expressly requires employers to provide leave for the period of confinement and the six weeks immediately preceding it; this Court has consistently held that the term "confinement" must be interpreted expansively to include any physical condition arising from the reproductive process. Menstruation, being an integral and cyclical part of that process, falls squarely within the protective ambit of the Act, and the State's Menstrual Leave Policy merely operationalises what the Act already mandates. Therefore, the State has a statutory duty to enforce the policy in all establishments, including the unorganized sector, and its omission attracts a writ of mandamus to compel compliance. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to proceed.

output.judge_response: Counsel, you rely on an expansive interpretation of 'confinement' under the Maternity Benefit Act — can you point to any judicial precedent that defines 'confinement' to include menstruation?
output.judge_notes: Counsel cited Maternity Benefit Act but no precedent for expansive interpretation; legally creative but thin.

output.judge_notes: Counsel dodged the question with generic principle, failed to provide the requested precedent.

input.case_summary: The Life Insurance Corporation of India (LIC) appealed a Gujarat High Court decision regarding its Table 58 policy, a convertible term insurance plan. The Consumer Education & Research Centre challenged the LIC's conditions for eligibility, arguing they were arbitrary and discriminatory, violating Articles 14, 19(1)(g), and 21 of the Constitution. The High Court struck down a condition limiting eligibility to those in government, quasi-government, or reputed commercial firms. The Supreme Court considered whether the LIC could restrict the policy to a specific class of individuals.
input.legal_issue: Whether the Life Insurance Corporation of India can restrict its convertible term insurance policy (Table 58) to salaried persons in government, quasi-government, or reputed commercial firms without violating Articles 14, 19(1)(g), and 21 of the Constitution of India.
input.relevant_laws: Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 19(1)(g) Constitution of India, Article 21 Constitution of India, Article 38 Constitution of India, Article 39 Constitution of India, Article 41 Constitution of India, Article 47 Constitution of India, Article 25 Universal Declaration of Human Rights, Article 7 International Covenant on Economic and Social Rights, Article 43 Constitution of India, Article 48-A Constitution of India, Article 39(e) Constitution of India
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the classification in LIC's Table 58 policy may indeed create a distinction between salaried employees of government, quasi-government, and reputed commercial firms versus other individuals. The counsel concedes that this distinction could be viewed as lacking a precise actuarial basis, as risk assessment could theoretically be performed on an individual basis for all applicants. However, the counsel submits that such broad categorizations are permissible under Article 14 as a matter of administrative convenience, even if they sacrifice perfect equality. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to proceed.

output.judge_response: Counsel, if you concede that the classification lacks an actuarial basis, how does mere administrative convenience constitute a rational nexus sufficient to withstand Article 14 scrutiny?
output.judge_notes: Counsel conceded absence of actuarial basis but relied solely on administrative convenience — that is a weak foundation for Article 14 justification.

input.case_summary: The Life Insurance Corporation of India (LIC) appealed a Gujarat High Court decision regarding its Table 58 policy, a convertible term insurance plan. The Consumer Education & Research Centre challenged the LIC's conditions for eligibility, arguing they were arbitrary and discriminatory, violating Articles 14, 19(1)(g), and 21 of the Constitution. The High Court struck down a condition limiting eligibility to those in government, quasi-government, or reputed commercial firms. The Supreme Court considered whether the LIC could restrict the policy to a specific class of individuals.
input.legal_issue: Whether the Life Insurance Corporation of India can restrict its convertible term insurance policy (Table 58) to salaried persons in government, quasi-government, or reputed commercial firms without violating Articles 14, 19(1)(g), and 21 of the Constitution of India.
input.relevant_laws: Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 19(1)(g) Constitution of India, Article 21 Constitution of India, Article 38 Constitution of India, Article 39 Constitution of India, Article 41 Constitution of India, Article 47 Constitution of India, Article 25 Universal Declaration of Human Rights, Article 7 International Covenant on Economic and Social Rights, Article 43 Constitution of India, Article 48-A Constitution of India, Article 39(e) Constitution of India
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, if you concede that the classification lacks an actuarial basis, how does mere administrative convenience constitute a rational nexus sufficient to withstand Article 14 scrutiny?
input.current_argument: My Lords, in response to the question posed by the Hon'ble bench, the counsel humbly submits that administrative convenience does not stand alone; it is underpinned by a legitimate state interest in ensuring the financial viability and orderly administration of a welfare-oriented insurance scheme. The classification here is based on a rational criterion—stable, verifiable income streams from government, quasi-government, or reputed commercial entities—which directly serves the purpose of minimizing risk and preventing adverse selection. This is not a case of arbitrary exclusion but a reasonable classification that has a clear nexus to the object sought to be achieved, namely the successful operation of the convertible term insurance plan. Therefore, even conceding the absence of an actuarial basis, the classification is sustainable under Article 14 as it is founded on a rational, non-discriminatory ground.

The classification here is based on a rational criterion—stable, verifiable income streams from government, quasi-government, or reputed commercial entities—which directly serves the purpose of minimizing risk and preventing adverse selection. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel seeks to proceed: the mere assertion of a "rational criterion" of stable income streams does not, in law, satisfy the requirement of a reasonable nexus to the object of the plan, especially when the LIC itself has failed to produce any actuarial data or empirical evidence demonstrating that these excluded classes are inherently riskier. My Lords, the counsel draws the attention of the Hon'ble bench to the fact that administrative ease, without more, is an impermissible basis for denying fundamental rights under Articles 14 and 21. Much obliged, My Lords.

input.case_summary: The Life Insurance Corporation of India (LIC) appealed a Gujarat High Court decision regarding its Table 58 policy, a convertible term insurance plan. The Consumer Education & Research Centre challenged the LIC's conditions for eligibility, arguing they were arbitrary and discriminatory, violating Articles 14, 19(1)(g), and 21 of the Constitution. The High Court struck down a condition limiting eligibility to those in government, quasi-government, or reputed commercial firms. The Supreme Court considered whether the LIC could restrict the policy to a specific class of individuals.
input.legal_issue: Whether the Life Insurance Corporation of India can restrict its convertible term insurance policy (Table 58) to salaried persons in government, quasi-government, or reputed commercial firms without violating Articles 14, 19(1)(g), and 21 of the Constitution of India.
input.relevant_laws: Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 19(1)(g) Constitution of India, Article 21 Constitution of India, Article 38 Constitution of India, Article 39 Constitution of India, Article 41 Constitution of India, Article 47 Constitution of India, Article 25 Universal Declaration of Human Rights, Article 7 International Covenant on Economic and Social Rights, Article 43 Constitution of India, Article 48-A Constitution of India, Article 39(e) Constitution of India
input.judge_last_response: Counsel, you argue that LIC failed to produce actuarial data; but in economic regulations, the burden is on the challenger to show the classification is arbitrary. What is your submission on the standard of review?
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the standard of review in cases involving State action under Article 14 is not the deferential rational basis test but a heightened scrutiny, as this Hon’ble Court established in Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India. In that landmark precedent, the Court held that any classification by the State must be supported by an intelligible differentia bearing a rational nexus to the objective, and crucially, the burden of justification shifts to the State when the classification trenches upon fundamental rights. Here, the LIC’s Table 58 policy directly implicates the right to livelihood under Article 21, as insurance access is integral to economic security and social justice. Therefore, as an instrumentality of the State, the LIC bore the affirmative duty to produce actuarial evidence proving that restricting eligibility to government and reputed commercial employees was necessary—not merely convenient. Their failure to offer such data before the High Court cannot be cured by post-hoc rationalizations, and this Court must hold that the classification fails constitutional muster. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the petitioner's response on the standard of review and the burden of justification under Article 14.
output.judge_notes: Strong application of Maneka Gandhi and burden shifting, but didn't address whether mere invocation of Article 21 automatically triggers heightened scrutiny or if a threshold showing is required.

input.case_summary: The Life Insurance Corporation of India (LIC) appealed a Gujarat High Court decision regarding its Table 58 policy, a convertible term insurance plan. input.relevant_laws: Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 19(1)(g) Constitution of India, Article 21 Constitution of India, Article 38 Constitution of India, Article 39 Constitution of India, Article 41 Constitution of India, Article 47 Constitution of India, Article 25 Universal Declaration of Human Rights, Article 7 International Covenant on Economic and Social Rights, Article 43 Constitution of India, Article 48-A Constitution of India, Article 39(e) Constitution of India

If a private insurer may lawfully restrict a policy to a particular class, there is no reason why the LIC, acting as a prudent market participant, should be denied that same liberty. The High Court's approach, if upheld, would transform every commercial term sheet into a constitutional question, completely ignoring the distinction between economic regulation and social welfare adjudication. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the respondent on the actuarial basis for the classification.
output.judge_notes: Completely dodged the actuarial evidence question — argued general principle instead, weak handling.

input.case_summary: This case involved a challenge to the constitutional validity of the Bhopal Gas Leak Disaster (Processing of Claims) Act, 1985. The Act was enacted to allow the Indian government to represent the victims of the Bhopal gas tragedy in legal proceedings against Union Carbide. Petitioners argued the Act violated fundamental rights and principles of natural justice. The Supreme Court upheld the Act's validity, finding it a reasonable measure to protect the victims' interests, but emphasized the need for adequate notice to victims before any settlement.
input.legal_issue: Whether the Bhopal Gas Leak Disaster (Processing of Claims) Act, 1985, which authorized the Central Government to represent victims of the Bhopal gas tragedy, is constitutionally valid, particularly in light of Articles 14, 19, and 21 of the Constitution of India and principles of natural justice.
input.relevant_laws: Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 19 Constitution of India, Article 21 Constitution of India, Article 38 Constitution of India, Article 39 Constitution of India, Article 39A Constitution of India, Article 48A Constitution of India, Article 51(g) Constitution of India, Bhopal Gas Leak Disaster (Processing of claims) Act, 1985, Section 3 Bhopal Gas Leak Disaster (Processing of claims) Act, 1985, Section 4 Bhopal Gas Leak Disaster (Processing of claims) Act, 1985, Section 5 Bhopal Gas Leak Disaster (Processing of claims) Act, 1985, Section 9 Bhopal Gas Leak Disaster (Processing of claims) Act, 1985, Section 11 Bhopal Gas Leak Disaster (Processing of claims) Act, 1985, Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order I Rule 8 Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order 23 Rule 3B Code of Civil Procedure, 1908
input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the Bhopal Gas Leak Disaster (Processing of Claims) Act, 1985, is unconstitutional as it violates the doctrine of separation of powers by vesting adjudicatory functions in the Central Government without providing for independent judicial review of individual claim determinations. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel draws attention to the fact that this executive usurpation of the judiciary's role undermines the rule of law and the basic structure of the Constitution. The counsel argues that such conferment of quasi-judicial authority upon the sovereign is impermissible under Articles 14 and 21, as it denies victims a fair and impartial forum for their claims. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: Counsel, your submission on separation of powers is intriguing — but does the Act actually vest adjudicatory power in the government, or does it merely process claims subject to court approval? Please clarify.
output.judge_notes: Petitioner raises separation of powers but fails to show actual ouster of judicial review; needs to engage with the Act's provisions.

output.judge_notes: Counsel answered directly but relied on a characterization of court approval as formal without addressing its actual scope — needs to show it's not de novo.

input.legal_issue: Whether the Bhopal Gas Leak Disaster (Processing of Claims) Act, 1985, which authorized the Central Government to represent victims of the Bhopal gas tragedy, is constitutionally valid, particularly in light of Articles 14, 19, and 21 of the Constitution of India and principles of natural justice.
input.relevant_laws: Article 14 Constitution of India, Article 19 Constitution of India, Article 21 Constitution of India, Article 38 Constitution of India, Article 39 Constitution of India, Article 39A Constitution of India, Article 48A Constitution of India, Article 51(g) Constitution of India, Bhopal Gas Leak Disaster (Processing of claims) Act, 1985, Section 3 Bhopal Gas Leak Disaster (Processing of claims) Act, 1985, Section 4 Bhopal Gas Leak Disaster (Processing of claims) Act, 1985, Section 5 Bhopal Gas Leak Disaster (Processing of claims) Act, 1985, Section 9 Bhopal Gas Leak Disaster (Processing of claims) Act, 1985, Section 11 Bhopal Gas Leak Disaster (Processing of claims) Act, 1985, Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order I Rule 8 Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order 23 Rule 3B Code of Civil Procedure, 1908

input.current_argument: My Lords, the counsel humbly submits that the initial negotiation phase taints the process because it fundamentally strips the victims of their right to be heard under Article 21, as this Hon'ble Court held in Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India—any procedure affecting life and liberty must be fair, just, and reasonable. The victims are excluded from negotiations that determine the very framework of settlement, and no subsequent independent assessment by the Commission under Section 9 can retroactively restore their right to participate at this critical stage. If Your Lordships are satisfied, the counsel draws attention to the core legal principle: procedural fairness under Article 14 and 21 requires participation at every stage, not merely a final review. The obvious counterargument that the Commission’s plenary power cures any defect fails because it ignores that the victims’ fundamental right to choose their own representation and to be heard during settlement negotiations is irretrievably lost. Much obliged, My Lords.

output.judge_response: The bench will now hear the respondent on whether the Commission's plenary power under Section 9 remedies any procedural infirmity in the negotiation stage.
output.judge_notes: Strong answer—directly addressed the question, invoked Maneka Gandhi correctly, and dismantled the Commission-as-cure argument with the irretrievable loss of participation right.
